3. The browser navigates to group_detail.html with groupProgramId set as a URL parameter (e.g., group_detail.html?groupProgramId=...).
4. The user continues registration or gets more information on group_detail.html (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Clear or modify filters without losing URL-based state
1. After applying some filters, the user decides to broaden the search (e.g., remove format filter or change month).
2. The user interacts with the filter UI (clears or changes specific filter controls).
3. The filter component updates the URL query string to remove or change only the affected parameters while preserving the others.
4. The search state manager reconstructs the filters object from the new URL state and calls searchGroupPrograms.
5. The results list updates accordingly, ensuring that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same filtered state.
interactions:
Interaction: Topic filter selection
- Trigger: User selects a topic option from a dropdown or set of controls.
- Effect on state: The selected topic’s value (from getGroupFilterOptions.topics) is stored as the current topic filter and written to the URL as topic=<value> (replacing any previous topic parameter).
- Data call: The search state manager constructs filters.topic from the new value and calls searchGroupPrograms with updated filters.
- UI update: Results list refreshes according to the new topic filter.

Interaction: Start month selection
- Trigger: User picks a start month from options sourced from getGroupFilterOptions.upcomingMonths.
- Effect on state: The chosen month’s value is stored as the current startMonth and written into the URL as startMonth=<value>.
- Data call: The search state manager uses the selected month’s startDate and endDate from upcomingMonths to set filters.startDateFrom and filters.startDateTo, then calls searchGroupPrograms.
- UI update: Results list shows only group programs with start dates in the selected month; selected month appears active in the filter controls.

Interaction: Format filter selection
- Trigger: User selects a format option (e.g., in_person, online, hybrid) from controls populated with getGroupFilterOptions.formats.
- Effect on state: The selected format’s value is stored as the current format filter and written into the URL as format=<value>. Clearing the format control removes the format parameter from the URL.
- Data call: The search state manager sets filters.format accordingly and calls searchGroupPrograms.
- UI update: Results list refreshes to show group programs matching the selected format.

Interaction: Clear all filters
- Trigger: User clicks a “Clear filters” control.
- Effect on state: All filter state for topic, startMonth, and format is reset to undefined, and the corresponding URL parameters are removed from the query string.
- Data call: searchGroupPrograms is called with a filters object that omits topic, format, and start date constraints (or uses a non-restrictive date range, depending on backend expectations).
- UI update: Filter controls return to their default unselected state and results list shows the broader set of group programs.

Interaction: Results list item click (open details)
- Trigger: User clicks on a group program card or its “View details” link within the results list.
- Effect on state: The click handler reads the bound GroupProgram.id from that list item.
- Navigation: Browser navigates to group_detail.html?groupProgramId=<GroupProgram.id>.
- The current filters remain in the groups.html URL, so if the user navigates back, the page can reconstruct the same results.

Interaction: Handling loading and error states
- Trigger: Any call to getGroupFilterOptions or searchGroupPrograms.
- Effect on state: A loading flag is set when a request starts and cleared when it finishes; if the request fails, an error state is set for that component.
- UI update: While loading, the results list is replaced or overlaid with a loading indicator component; on error, an error message component appears with an option to retry (which triggers the same data call again).

Interaction: URL changes via browser navigation (back/forward)
- Trigger: User uses the browser’s back or forward buttons after modifying filters.
- Effect on state: The page reads the new URL query parameters (topic, startMonth, format) triggered by the navigation event.
- Data call: The search state manager rebuilds the filters object from the URL and calls searchGroupPrograms.
- UI update: Filters and results list update to match the historical URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic: URL parameters (topic, startMonth, format) are the primary source of truth for filter state on this page. On initial page load and on any browser navigation event, the page parses these parameters: 1) topic: if present, it is matched against the values in getGroupFilterOptions.topics. If a match is found, that topic is selected; if not, the topic filter is cleared without causing an error. 2) format: if present, it is matched against the values in getGroupFilterOptions.formats. If it does not match any known format, the format filter is cleared. 3) startMonth: if present, the page first checks getGroupFilterOptions.upcomingMonths for an entry whose value equals startMonth. If found, filters.startDateFrom and filters.startDateTo are taken directly from that entry’s startDate and endDate when calling searchGroupPrograms. If not found, the page interprets startMonth as a year-month identifier, derives the first and last day of that month, and uses them for filters.startDateFrom and filters.startDateTo, while the month selector is either set to a special 'custom' state or left unselected. When any filter is changed via the UI, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (adding, changing, or removing them) using the current filter state, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. After each URL update, the page recomputes the filters object used by searchGroupPrograms, ensuring that a page refresh or sharing the link reproduces the same filter state and results. The response from searchGroupPrograms (an array of objects with groupProgram, firstSessionStartDate, hasEveningSession) is stored only in memory and bound to the results list; it is not written back into the URL.

components.functionality: On page load and on browser navigation events, reads URL parameters, loads filter options via getGroupFilterOptions, and establishes the initial filter state (topic, startMonth, format) used by other components.
Displays and manages the interactive filters for topic, start month, and format using options from getGroupFilterOptions and the current URL-derived filter state.

Listens for filter changes (including those resulting from URL updates), builds the filters object for searchGroupPrograms (including resolving startMonth into startDateFrom/startDateTo), triggers searches, and manages loading and error states for the results.
Shows a summary of the current search context (such as number of results returned and which filters are active) based on the latest searchGroupPrograms response and current filter state.

Renders the list of group programs returned by searchGroupPrograms, each item including key fields from groupProgram and derived values like firstSessionStartDate and hasEveningSession, and exposes actions to open details.
Monitors the current results array from searchGroupPrograms and shows an empty-state message and basic guidance when no group programs match the active filters.
Displays a loading state whenever a call to searchGroupPrograms is in progress, based on loading flags controlled by the group-search-state-manager.
Displays error information if getGroupFilterOptions or searchGroupPrograms fails, and provides a retry control that triggers the corresponding data request again with the existing filter state.
Synchronizes filter state changes with the URL query string without using localStorage, and notifies other components when the URL changes so that they can recompute filters and refresh results.
Receives the selected GroupProgram.id from user interactions on the group-program-list and navigates to group_detail.html with groupProgramId set as a URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit responsive grid, likely a 12-column layout with a centered max-width content container (approx. 1080–1200px) and full-bleed hero image band.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, single-column flow as the mockup: global header at the top, then a full-width hero band with a centered "Group Programs" title, and beneath that a light, softly tinted main content area. Inside the main content, components are stacked vertically in this order: filters initializer (non-visual), filter bar, search state management (non-visual), results summary, the results region (which alternates between list, empty state, loading, or error), and finally the non-visual routing/navigation helpers. The visible pieces (filter bar, summary, list/status) align to a centered content column, maintaining the calm, editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, single-column flow as the mockup: global header at the top, then a full-width hero band with a centered "Group Programs" title, and beneath that a light, softly tinted main content area. Inside the main content, components are stacked vertically in this order: filters initializer (non-visual), filter bar, search state management (non-visual), results summary, the results region (which alternates between list, empty state, loading, or error), and finally the non-visual routing/navigation helpers. The visible pieces (filter bar, summary, list/status) align to a centered content column, maintaining the calm, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header takes a modest vertical height with generous padding, the hero occupies a prominent band roughly 30–40% of the viewport height, and the main content begins with a comfortable 48–64px gap under the hero. Within the main content, a central column spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with balanced left and right margins of soft whitespace. Vertical spacing between major blocks (filter bar, summary, results area) follows 24–48px increments, preserving a spacious rhythm, while individual list items use moderate internal padding to remain readable but uncluttered.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. The filter bar presents only a few clear controls in a single row on desktop (stacked on smaller screens) with 12–16px internal padding, and no cramped, multi-line labels. The results summary is a single, concise line. The program list is a vertical stack with generous 16–24px gaps between items and comfortable line height, avoiding dense grids or side-by-side cards. Empty, loading, and error states each occupy the same results area with plenty of breathing room around their messages, ensuring the page never feels busy or overwhelming.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s gentle Z-then-vertical pattern: the user’s eye moves from the logo left to the nav right, then drops into the hero where the centered "Group Programs" title is the main focal point. From there, the eye continues downward into the main content, first encountering the filter bar as the primary interactive focus, then the subtle results summary just beneath it, and finally the vertical program list or status message. Within the list, attention flows top-to-bottom through each program entry, with navigation to details feeling like a natural continuation of that downward reading path rather than a disruptive jump.

layout.overall_description: Below the calm, right-aligned navigation header and a full-width hero with a softly blurred, nature-inspired background and a centered "Group Programs" title, the main content sits on a pale green-tinted panel that feels gently distinct from the hero. A single, centered column—about two-thirds of the page width on desktop—holds all interactive elements, framed by generous whitespace on both sides. At the top of this column, the filter bar forms a low-profile control strip with a few well-spaced dropdowns for topic, start month, and format. Just beneath, a subtle one-line summary reflects the active filters, followed by a vertically stacked results region where either a list of group programs appears as soft, card-like rows, or a calm empty/loading/error message occupies the same space. Non-visual state and routing helpers sit behind this layout, enabling smooth filtering and navigation without adding visual clutter, preserving the quiet, reassuring feel of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This initializer component sits logically at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the hero section but above the visible filter bar, without rendering any visible UI. It occupies no actual height or width and does not disrupt the vertical spacing; instead, it runs before the filter bar is drawn, ensuring that when the user first sees the central column, the filters already match any URL/query defaults. Spatially, you can imagine it as a hidden layer aligned with the left edge of the content column, fully transparent and zero-height within the stacked layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic component; occupies no visual space and does not affect layout beyond ensuring the filter bar initializes with correct state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is the first visible interactive element in the main content column, positioned directly beneath the hero with a comfortable 48–64px vertical gap so it does not crowd the page title. It is horizontally centered and spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, matching the main text column, with soft left and right margins. Within the bar, 2–4 filter controls (e.g., Topic, Start Month, Format) are laid out in a single row from left to right on desktop with even spacing and 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding inside the bar, giving it a low-profile, pill-like or subtle-card feel on the pale green background. On smaller screens, these controls stack vertically, maintaining the same left alignment and generous 16–24px spacing between controls to preserve the calm, readable density. There is a 24–32px gap between the bottom edge of the filter bar and the results summary below, keeping the layout airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow; scrolls with the page. On smaller viewports, internal layout responds to width by stacking controls vertically while keeping the overall bar centered in the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search state manager conceptually wraps the filter bar, summary, and results region as an invisible container aligned to the same central column. It does not render any visible elements or occupy noticeable vertical space; instead, it ensures that changes in the filter bar immediately update the summary and the results area below. Spatially, it behaves like a transparent frame around the middle section of the page, extending from just above the filter bar down through the bottom of the results list or status message, without introducing extra margins or padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller wrapping other components; no direct spatial footprint but governs how visible components within the central column update and re-render.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is a slim text bar placed directly beneath the filter bar, aligned to the left edge of the main content column with a 24–32px vertical gap separating it from the filter controls. It spans the full width of the content column (about 60–70% of the viewport), but the text itself is concise and set in a calm, body-weight style, such as "Showing 4 groups for Anxiety in March · Online & In-person." The line height is comfortable, and there is 16–24px of space between the bottom of the summary and the start of the results area, reinforcing a clear hierarchy while keeping this element visually understated. It reads as a gentle subheading that orients the user without competing with the hero title or the filter bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline text within the main flow; expands vertically only as needed for one or two lines but otherwise keeps a minimal height to maintain an unobtrusive presence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group program list occupies the main content block below the results summary, beginning approximately 16–24px beneath it and extending downward as a single, vertical column. It follows the width of the central content area, so each program entry spans nearly the full width of that column (around 60–70% of the viewport), with soft off-white or lightly tinted backgrounds that subtly distinguish each item from the pale green page background without heavy borders. Each program row has generous internal padding—around 16–24px on all sides—and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows, creating a breathable stack of cards that are easy to scan one by one. Text within each item is left-aligned, with the group title at the top, a short description, schedule, and format details, all using ample line height. The entire list scrolls naturally with the page, and on smaller screens the same single-column stack is preserved, simply adjusting to the device width while maintaining the same relaxed spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding list within the main flow; list height grows or shrinks depending on the number of programs, pushing the rest of the page content downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no matching programs, the empty state appears in place of the program list, occupying the same central results area that begins 16–24px below the results summary. It is centered within the content column, with the main message left-aligned but visually centered horizontally by equal side margins, and substantial vertical padding (32–48px) around the text so the section feels open and non-alarming. Optionally, a small, soft illustration or icon can appear above the main message, with 16–24px spacing between them, all confined to the same column width as the list. The surrounding whitespace above and below this block mirrors the breathing room of several list items, making the page feel consistent even when there are no results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the program list, loading indicator, and error display; it takes over the results region but does not alter the position of the filter bar or summary above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: During loading, a simple, calm loading indicator (such as a small spinner or progress animation) is displayed in the same results region below the summary, replacing the list or empty state. The indicator is horizontally centered within the content column and positioned with generous top and bottom padding (32–48px) so it floats in a clear, open space rather than cramped against other elements. There is no dark overlay; the background remains the pale green of the main content area, preserving the gentle visual tone while clearly signaling that content is on the way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the list, empty state, and error display; occupies only the vertical space needed for the indicator and its surrounding padding, and scrolls naturally with the page without fixing to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs, an error display replaces the results list in the same central area beneath the summary. It appears as a full-width banner or card within the content column, aligned to the left edge and spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width, with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px of vertical spacing from the summary above. The message is concise and left-aligned, possibly with a slightly stronger weight for the main line (e.g., "We’re having trouble loading group programs") and a calmer secondary line offering guidance or a retry action. Any button or link (such as "Try again") is placed directly below the message with 12–16px spacing, all within the same card. The overall treatment remains understated, avoiding harsh colors or heavy outlines to keep the emotional tone supportive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the list, empty state, and loading indicator; it fills only the necessary vertical space in the results region and scrolls as part of the normal page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL query sync component is invisible and does not produce any visual elements, but is conceptually positioned alongside the filter bar within the central column’s logic layer. It ensures that changes to filters update the URL and that visiting a URL with specific query parameters pre-populates the filter bar and results. From a spatial standpoint, it has zero width and height and introduces no extra gaps; its presence is purely behavioral, keeping the visible layout clean and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering helper; no direct spatial footprint, interacts only with browser routing and state, leaving the physical arrangement of components unchanged.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group detail navigation component does not render its own visual UI but governs how users move from the list view to individual group detail pages. Functionally, it binds to each program row within the list so that the entire card area is a comfortable click or tap target, with consistent padding and perhaps a subtle right-facing chevron aligned along the right edge inside each card. Spatially, it lives within the same central column as the program list, adding no extra height or margins; it simply layers interactivity over the existing card layouts to make navigation feel natural and consistent with the vertical scan flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual navigation handler; no independent spatial presence, but it influences the interactive behavior of the program list items, which remain static elements in the vertical layout.

name: Group Details
title: Group Details
description: Displays full information about a selected group program and lets users choose a specific recurring session time slot to register for.

core_features: Read the groupProgramId from the URL and load full group details by calling getGroupDetails(groupProgramId).
Display key group program information (title, topic, format, location, fees, total number of sessions, facilitators, start date) from the GroupProgram data.
Show detailed program description and goals using GroupProgram.description and GroupProgram.goals.
List all related GroupSession entries for the program, including weekday, start_time, end_time, timezone, capacity, registered_count, date range, and evening status.
Highlight evening sessions (where GroupSession.is_evening is true), including those that run between 18:00 and 20:00 on weekday evenings, within the schedule listing.
Allow the user to select exactly one specific GroupSession as their chosen time slot via a radio-button list or a dropdown selector populated from the sessions data.
Indicate which sessions are full or inactive based on GroupSession.capacity, GroupSession.registered_count, and GroupSession.is_active, and prevent selection of non-available sessions.
Provide a Register / Reserve a Spot action that, when triggered with a valid selected GroupSession, navigates to group_registration.html with the selected groupSessionId in the URL.
Handle loading and error states for getGroupDetails, including missing or invalid groupProgramId in the URL.
Optionally show informational messaging when no active GroupSession entries are available for the selected GroupProgram.
user_workflows:
View group details and schedule:
1. User navigates to group_detail.html with a groupProgramId URL parameter.
2. The page reads groupProgramId from the URL.
3. The page calls getGroupDetails(groupProgramId).
4. While the API call is in progress, the page shows a loading state.
5. On success, the page displays the GroupProgram information (title, topic, format, location, fee_per_session, total_sessions, facilitators, start_date, description, goals).
6. The page then lists all associated GroupSession entries, showing for each: weekday, start_time, end_time, timezone (if provided), date range (start_date/end_date when available), available capacity (capacity minus registered_count when both are present), and evening indicator (is_evening).
7. If there are no sessions or no active sessions, the page shows an inline message indicating that no time slots are currently available.

Select a session time slot:
1. After the group details and sessions load, the user reviews the list of GroupSession options.
2. For each session that is active (is_active is true) and not full (capacity and registered_count indicate availability, or capacity is not set), the page renders an enabled radio button or a selectable option in a dropdown.
3. Sessions that are inactive (is_active is false) or full (capacity and registered_count indicate no remaining spots) are clearly marked and their radio/dropdown options are disabled.
4. The user clicks a radio button or chooses a session from the dropdown to select their preferred time slot.
5. The selected GroupSession.id is stored in page state as the currently chosen groupSessionId.

Register for a selected session:
1. After selecting a preferred GroupSession, the user clicks the Register / Reserve a Spot button.
2. The page checks if a groupSessionId is currently selected.
3. If no session is selected, the page shows an inline validation message and does not navigate away.
4. If a valid session is selected, the page constructs a URL to group_registration.html including the chosen groupSessionId as a URL parameter (e.g., group_registration.html?groupSessionId=[selected_id]).
5. The browser navigates to the constructed group_registration.html URL so the user can complete the registration form for that specific time slot.

Handle missing or invalid groupProgramId:
1. If group_detail.html is loaded without a groupProgramId URL parameter, the page cannot call getGroupDetails.
2. The page shows an error state explaining that the requested group cannot be found, and may provide a link back to the group listing page (groups.html) using standard navigation.
3. If groupProgramId is present but getGroupDetails returns an error (e.g., group not found or not active), the page displays an error message and does not show any session selection or registration controls.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the groupProgramId URL parameter and triggers getGroupDetails(groupProgramId).
While getGroupDetails is pending, the user sees a loading indicator and cannot interact with session selection or registration controls.
If getGroupDetails fails, the user sees a non-interactive error message and no session selection or registration controls.
When getGroupDetails succeeds, the user can scroll the page, read the group description, goals, and other metadata, and view the list of sessions.

For each available GroupSession, the user can click a radio button option or select it from a dropdown to mark it as their chosen time slot; selecting a different option updates the selected groupSessionId in page state.
Sessions that are full, inactive, or otherwise not available have their corresponding radio/dropdown options disabled; clicking them has no effect.
Clicking the Register / Reserve a Spot button with no session selected triggers inline validation and shows an error message near the selection control; the page remains on group_detail.html.

Clicking the Register / Reserve a Spot button with a valid selected session constructs a URL that includes groupSessionId and performs a client-side navigation to group_registration.html with that parameter.

Optional interaction: the user can refresh the page or change the URL’s groupProgramId parameter (e.g., via browser address bar or external link), causing the page to re-run getGroupDetails for the new groupProgramId and update all bound components.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the groupProgramId URL parameter. On every load of group_detail.html, the page reads groupProgramId from the query string. If groupProgramId is present, the page calls getGroupDetails(groupProgramId). The response provides groupProgram (GroupProgram) and sessions (array of GroupSession). These objects are stored in in-memory page state and bound to the components. If groupProgramId is missing or empty, the page does not call getGroupDetails and instead enters a 'missing ID' error state. The page also maintains transient in-memory state for: (1) loading status for getGroupDetails (e.g., 'loading', 'loaded', 'error'), (2) error details (if the call fails), and (3) the currently selected groupSessionId, initially unset. The selected groupSessionId is updated when the user changes the session selection control. It is not persisted to the URL or any storage on this page; it is only used to construct the navigation URL to group_registration.html when the user clicks Register. For navigation out of this page, the page constructs the URL group_registration.html?groupSessionId=[selected_groupSessionId] when a valid session is selected. The registration page can then read groupSessionId from its own query string. All logic about session availability is computed dynamically from the GroupSession properties: a session is considered 'selectable' when is_active is true and either capacity is not specified or registered_count is not specified or registered_count is less than capacity. Sessions outside this availability condition are rendered but with disabled selection controls. Evening sessions (is_evening true) are flagged and displayed as such, including those that fall between typical evening hours such as 18:00–20:00 on weekdays; the actual display uses the session's weekday, start_time, and end_time values without hardcoded time ranges.

components.functionality: Reads the groupProgramId from the URL, calls getGroupDetails(groupProgramId), manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded GroupProgram and sessions data to child components.
Displays the basic identifying information for the group program, such as title and topic, and may include the format and high-level location context.

Shows key metadata about the group program, including format, location, fee_per_session, total_sessions, start_date, and facilitator_names when available. It presents this information in a structured list for quick scanning.
Renders the GroupProgram.description and GroupProgram.goals to explain what the group covers, who it is for, and what participants can expect to work on.

Displays all GroupSession entries associated with the loaded GroupProgram as a structured list or table, including weekday, start_time, end_time, timezone (if present), date range (start_date and end_date when present), capacity, registered_count, and an indicator for evening sessions (is_evening). It calculates and displays availability status (e.g., spots remaining, full, inactive) based on the session fields and indicates which sessions are available to select.

Provides the interactive control that lets the user choose exactly one GroupSession as their preferred time slot. It can be implemented as either a radio-button group aligned with the session list or a dropdown whose options are sourced from the available sessions. Only sessions considered available (is_active true and not full) are enabled options. When the user selects an option, it updates the current selected groupSessionId in page state.

For each session in the schedule list, computes and shows an availability label based on capacity and registered_count (for example, available, limited spots, or full) and reflects is_active status. It also visually notes when a session is an evening session (is_evening true). This component is typically rendered inline within each session row.

Displays inline validation feedback if the user attempts to register without selecting a session. It listens for validation events from the registration action and shows or hides an appropriate message without using any hardcoded session details.

Contains the primary Register / Reserve a Spot button. When clicked, it checks whether a valid groupSessionId is selected; if not, it triggers the session-selection-error component. If a valid session is selected, it constructs a URL to group_registration.html with the selected groupSessionId as a query parameter and initiates navigation.

Shows an informational message when getGroupDetails returns no sessions or no active sessions for the group program, indicating that there are currently no available time slots to register for. It is conditionally displayed based on the sessions data.

Displays an error message when group details cannot be loaded, either because groupProgramId is missing/invalid or because getGroupDetails returned an error. It may optionally include a link back to the group listing page (groups.html) implemented as a normal navigation link.

layout.grid_system: Implicit responsive grid, likely a 12-column layout with a centered max-width content container (approx. 1080–1200px) and full-bleed hero image band.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, vertically stacked content column centered within a fixed max-width container (approx. 1080–1200px). Above this, the global header and a full-width hero band (with the existing leaf imagery and a simple page/group title) remain consistent with the mockup. Within the main content column, the components flow in a linear top-to-bottom order: group header → group meta info → group description/goals → schedule list (with inline selection control and availability indicator) → validation message → register action bar. Loader, empty, and error states temporarily replace or overlay the schedule section but do not change the overall linear structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, vertically stacked content column centered within a fixed max-width container (approx. 1080–1200px). Above this, the global header and a full-width hero band (with the existing leaf imagery and a simple page/group title) remain consistent with the mockup. Within the main content column, the components flow in a linear top-to-bottom order: group header → group meta info → group description/goals → schedule list (with inline selection control and availability indicator) → validation message → register action bar. Loader, empty, and error states temporarily replace or overlay the schedule section but do not change the overall linear structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, centered, with soft whitespace on either side. Vertical spacing between major sections (header/meta/description/schedule/action) follows a 32–64px rhythm based on the 8px grid, preserving the airy, calm feel. The schedule list area takes slightly more vertical space than the description to emphasize its interactive importance, but no section feels cramped. On smaller screens, the column becomes nearly full-width with 16–24px side padding while preserving generous vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Each section has low visual density: short text blocks, clear headings, and ample line height. The meta info is presented as concise lines rather than dense tables; the description and goals are broken into small paragraphs and bullet points; the session list uses well-separated rows with comfortable padding (12–16px vertical per row) and clear breathing room between controls. Error and status messages are single, short lines with modest spacing around them so they are noticeable but not overwhelming.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the global header and nav, down to the centered hero title (e.g., the group name), then continues into the main column: first the group header (title and key descriptor), then gently down through supporting meta details, then into the more narrative description/goals. From there the focus shifts to the interactive area—session schedule rows, with selection controls anchored on the left and availability indicators on the right, forming a clear horizontal cue in each row. Below the list, any selection validation message appears directly above the primary register action bar, which anchors the interaction at the bottom of the main column. Loader and error/empty states temporarily become the primary focal point in the middle of the column, replacing the schedule block while maintaining the same flow and alignment.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing site header and a full-width hero band with the familiar blurred leaf background, the Group Details page content sits in a calm, centered column against a very light green-tinted background. The column is comfortably narrow—about two-thirds of the viewport on desktop—with wide margins that create a quiet frame around the content. At the top of this column, the group’s name and key header information stand out as the primary anchor, set in a slightly larger type size and left-aligned. Just beneath, succinct lines of meta information (such as age range, format, location, and fee) are stacked vertically with gentle spacing, forming a compact but airy block. A bit further down, a description and goals section flows as short paragraphs and bullet points, breathing easily with generous line height. Below this narrative area, the schedule section becomes the interactive heart of the page: a vertical list of available session times, each row spanning the width of the content column. On each row, a subtle selection control on the left and a soft availability pill on the right frame the session details in the center. Any selection error message sits calmly below this list, followed by a wide, prominent but understated register action bar at the bottom of the column, encouraging completion without shouting. When data is loading, a centered loader appears in the middle of the column; if there are no sessions or an error occurs, a gentle empty or error state replaces the schedule list in that same space, keeping the layout stable and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears in the central content column, occupying roughly the middle third of the visible content area. It is horizontally centered within the max-width container and vertically spaced with about 64px of margin above and below to keep it visually isolated. On initial page load, it sits directly below the hero band where the group header and schedule area would usually begin, effectively replacing the main content. In partial loads (e.g., just the sessions), it overlays or replaces the schedule list block only, positioned where the session list would appear—below the group description and above the register action bar—while preserving the same left alignment and column width. The loader graphic or spinner is modest in size, surrounded by ample whitespace to match the low-density, calm aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally inline within the main column; can optionally behave as a lightweight overlay over the schedule section, centered both horizontally and in the vertical space of that section, dimming only the schedule block but not the global header or hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group header is the first component in the main content column beneath the hero band, aligned with the content’s left edge. It spans the full width of the central column (about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop) and includes the group title as the dominant text element, possibly in a serif typeface to echo the site’s hero headings. There is generous top padding (about 48–64px from the bottom of the hero) so the header doesn’t crowd the image above, and about 16–24px of padding below to separate it from the meta information block. Any small lead-in text (e.g., category, age range label) appears above or just below the title in a lighter weight, but still left-aligned. Overall, this block forms a clear, strong starting anchor for the page’s vertical reading path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the main column, maintaining its position relative to the hero band on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group meta info sits directly below the group header, left-aligned to the same column edge and spanning the same width. It forms a compact vertical stack of 2–6 short lines (e.g., 'Format: In-person', 'Location: Downtown Office', 'Duration: 8 weeks'), each line separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. There is a modest 16px gap between this block and the group header above, and a larger 24–32px gap before the description/goals section below, visually grouping the header and meta together while still creating a clear break to the narrative content. The overall height of this block is relatively small compared to the description and schedule sections, maintaining a light, low-density feel. On mobile, it remains a single-column stack with slightly increased line spacing to ensure legibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section that scales with content height; it does not scroll independently or use any special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description/goals section begins just below the meta info, separated by around 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the content column, left-aligned, with internal padding of about 16–24px on top and bottom if presented on a subtly tinted panel, or simply extra margin if rendered directly on the page background. A short heading such as 'About this Group' or 'What to Expect' appears at the top, followed by 1–3 brief paragraphs and then a small bullet list for specific goals or topics. Line height is generous, and each bullet has at least 8px of vertical separation to keep the section light. The bottom of this block has a more pronounced margin (32–40px) before the schedule list, signaling a transition from information to interaction while preserving the seamless vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text section; it grows vertically with content but remains within the main column with no special scrolling or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session schedule list occupies a distinct section below the description/goals, starting after a vertical gap of about 32–40px. It spans the width of the central column and is left-aligned, with a small heading such as 'Available Session Times' at the top. Below the heading, individual session rows are stacked in a single column, each row spanning nearly the full column width with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside the row area. Rows have soft separation—either subtle dividers or 8–12px vertical gaps—so they feel distinct but not card-like or heavy. Within each row, the session time and recurrence details are centered horizontally between the selection control on the far left and the availability indicator on the far right, creating a calm horizontal structure. The list height can grow with more sessions, pushing the register action bar downward while maintaining consistent left alignment and comfortable whitespace on all sides. On small screens, the rows become slightly taller, but the layout remains a single column with the same left-right structure inside each row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable as part of the main page flow; if the list becomes very long, the page scroll handles the length while the register action bar can optionally remain sticky near the bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session selection control is integrated within each row of the session schedule list, positioned at the far left edge of the row content. It appears as a radio button, checkbox, or similarly minimal control, vertically centered within the row’s height so it aligns with the main session text. The control sits about 16px from the left padding of the row, with 8–12px of horizontal space between the control and the session details text, ensuring it is clearly associated but not cramped. On narrow screens, it maintains this left position, still aligned with the rest of the column, so the eye naturally scans from control → session details → availability indicator. If only a single selection is possible, only one control can be active at a time, but visually all controls share the same position and spacing across rows, creating a calm, orderly rhythm down the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each schedule row; it scrolls with the list and has no fixed behavior. On hover/focus, subtle state changes occur within the same spatial footprint without shifting surrounding content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session availability indicator appears within each session row on the far right side, forming a small pill or label (e.g., 'Open', 'Filling fast', 'Waitlist') that is horizontally aligned with the session details text and vertically centered within the row. It is right-aligned to the inner edge of the row’s padding, typically about 16–24px from the row’s right boundary. The indicator is visually subtle—using soft, nature-inspired colors that harmonize with the greens and off-whites—but still distinct enough to be scannable when the user’s eye runs down the right side of the list. The space between the main session text and this indicator remains at least 16–24px so the row doesn’t feel crowded. On smaller screens, if space becomes tight, the indicator may wrap to a second line directly beneath the main session text but still aligned to the right, preserving the left-to-right information hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each schedule row; it scrolls with the list and adjusts its vertical position if the row height grows, but does not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selection error message sits directly below the entire session schedule list section, aligned with the left edge of the content column. It occupies only a single line or small text block, with a compact height and about 8–12px of vertical margin above it to separate it from the last session row. When present, it appears just above the register action bar, making it clearly associated with the session selection and subsequent registration action. The text is concise and slightly more saturated in color (e.g., a muted red) but still fits the soft, gentle aesthetic. There is an additional 16–24px of margin below the error message before the register action bar to prevent visual crowding and keep the page’s calm rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, appearing only when validation fails. It pushes the register action bar down slightly but does not overlay or float; it remains part of the normal vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The register action bar anchors the bottom of the main content column, appearing directly below the session schedule (and any selection error message when present). It spans the full width of the central column and is left-aligned, with internal padding of about 16–24px top and bottom. The primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Register for this Group') is centered or left-aligned within this bar, sized generously but not oversized—about 40–48px tall with 16–24px horizontal padding—ensuring it feels clear yet gentle. To maintain the calm, non-pushy feel, the bar’s background may be only slightly differentiated (e.g., a very soft tint) from the page background. Above the bar there is roughly 24–32px of margin from the error/message or the last schedule row, and below there is enough space (32–48px) before the page footer or the end of the content to avoid any sense of the button being cramped against the bottom of the viewport. On longer pages, the user scrolls down to reach this bar; optionally, on desktop and mobile, the bar may become a light sticky element that stays visible near the bottom of the viewport once the user enters the schedule area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily a static block at the bottom of the main content column. Optionally can become a low-height sticky bar near the bottom of the viewport when the user has scrolled past the schedule list, maintaining its width and alignment but staying in view for easier registration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no available sessions, the no-sessions message replaces the entire session schedule list area in the main column. It appears in the same vertical position where the list heading and rows would normally be—below the description/goals section and above the register action bar—maintaining alignment with the left edge of the content column. The message is typically centered horizontally within this block and occupies about one to two lines of text, optionally accompanied by a small, subtle icon above or beside it. There is generous padding (24–32px) around the message, with at least 32px of margin from the description above and 24–32px before the register action bar below, creating a calm, contained empty state. The overall height of this block is moderate—enough to feel intentional but not so tall that it feels like a void.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the schedule list. It does not overlay other content; instead it takes the schedule list’s place in the natural vertical flow, pushing the register action bar downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The load error message appears when there is a failure fetching the group or schedule data. It is placed in the central content column, generally in the same position as the schedule section—just below the group header/meta (if available) or directly under the hero band if nothing else loaded. The message is left-aligned to the content column but may also be visually centered within a soft, lightly bordered panel that spans most of the column width. There is generous vertical spacing above and below the message (32–40px) to set it apart and make it easy to notice without feeling harsh. If retry controls or links are included, they appear immediately below the text, aligned left, with 16–24px of spacing. The register action bar is typically hidden or disabled when this message is present, so the user’s attention focuses on the error area in the middle of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline state that replaces the schedule list and related interactive components. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay the header or hero, keeping the overall layout consistent and predictable.

name: Group Registration
title: Group Registration
description: Page for confirming a selected group session, entering participant details, choosing a payment option, and submitting a group registration.

core_features: Read the groupSessionId URL parameter and load the registration context for that group session using getGroupRegistrationContext.

Display a summary of the selected group program, including title, format, location (if available), and a clear schedule using the weekday, start_time, end_time, and date fields from the GroupSession and GroupProgram.
Show current capacity information when available (capacity and registered_count) and calculate remaining seats to inform the participant count field.
Provide a registration form that collects participant_first_name, participant_last_name, and participant_email.

Allow the user to select the number of participants (num_participants), initialized from defaultNumParticipants returned by getGroupRegistrationContext, with validation against any remaining capacity when capacity is provided.
Display payment options dynamically based on allowedPaymentOptions from getGroupRegistrationContext, including deferred options such as pay_later or pay_at_first_session when available.
Validate required fields (names, email, num_participants, payment_option) and email format before submit, with inline error messages.
Submit the completed registration using submitGroupRegistration, passing groupSessionId, participantFirstName, participantLastName, participantEmail, numParticipants, and paymentOption.
Show success or error feedback based on submitGroupRegistration response, including message and any returned GroupRegistration information.
After successful registration, display a confirmation state with a link to navigate back to the site home (index.html) and keep the submitted details visible on the page.
user_workflows:
Registration from Group Details:
1. User clicks a registration link or button for a specific group session on group_detail.html; they are navigated to group_registration.html with the selected groupSessionId in the query string.
2. On page load, the Group Registration page reads groupSessionId from the URL.
3. The page calls getGroupRegistrationContext with that groupSessionId.
4. When the response returns, the page shows the program and session summary (including weekday and start/end time), capacity/remaining seats (if available), and initializes the registration form with defaultNumParticipants.
5. The user enters their first name, last name, and email address.
6. The user adjusts the number of participants (if needed) within allowed limits and selects a payment option from the dynamically generated list.
7. The user clicks the primary submit button (e.g., "Complete Registration").
8. The page validates all fields; if there are validation errors, error messages are shown and submission is blocked until corrected.
9. If validation passes, the page calls submitGroupRegistration with the form data and the current groupSessionId.
10. If submitGroupRegistration returns success=true, the page shows a confirmation message and a summary of the registration details. A link is presented to go back to the home page (index.html).
11. If submitGroupRegistration returns success=false, the page shows the message from the response as an error and keeps the form data so the user can adjust and retry.

Handling missing or invalid groupSessionId:
1. User lands on group_registration.html without a groupSessionId in the URL or with an invalid value.
2. On load, the page detects that groupSessionId is missing or cannot be used.
3. The page does not call getGroupRegistrationContext and instead shows an error state explaining that the session could not be loaded.
4. The registration form and submit button are disabled or hidden because there is no valid group session context.
5. A link is available for the user to return to the home page (index.html) or back to the originating page (handled by browser back).

Retrying after context load or submission errors:
1. If getGroupRegistrationContext fails (e.g., network or service error), the page shows an error message and a retry action.
2. When the user clicks retry, the page calls getGroupRegistrationContext again using the same groupSessionId from the URL.
3. If submitGroupRegistration fails with success=false, the error message from the response is displayed while keeping all previously entered form values.
4. The user may adjust their inputs (for example, reduce num_participants if capacity is insufficient or choose a different payment option if indicated) and click the submit button again to re-call submitGroupRegistration.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the groupSessionId query parameter from the URL and triggers a call to getGroupRegistrationContext; while waiting, a loading state is shown and the form is disabled.
If getGroupRegistrationContext succeeds, the program title, basic description, facilitators (if available), and schedule information are populated in a read-only summary component.
If the GroupSession includes capacity and registered_count, the page calculates remaining capacity and displays a text indicator and/or uses it to constrain the allowed values for num_participants.

The participant_first_name, participant_last_name, and participant_email fields are editable text inputs; they show validation messages when left empty or when email format is invalid upon blur or form submission.

The num_participants input is typically a numeric input or stepper; it is initialized to defaultNumParticipants from getGroupRegistrationContext and constrained to positive integers; if remaining capacity is known, values greater than remaining capacity produce a validation error.

Payment options are rendered as a list of selectable controls (e.g., radio buttons), one per entry in allowedPaymentOptions. The user selects exactly one; the selected code is stored and later passed as paymentOption.

Clicking the primary call-to-action button (e.g., "Complete Registration" or "Submit") triggers client-side validation; if validation passes, the button enters a submitting state and calls submitGroupRegistration with the current form values and groupSessionId.
While submitGroupRegistration is in progress, the submit button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions and progress feedback is shown.

If submitGroupRegistration returns success=true, the form component switches to a read-only confirmation state: fields are disabled or hidden, and the confirmation message plus details from registration are displayed.
If submitGroupRegistration returns success=false, the error message is surfaced in a status component; the form remains editable so the user can modify inputs and click submit again.

A link or button labeled for navigation to the home page (e.g., "Back to Home") is available in the confirmation state; when clicked, the browser navigates to index.html without additional URL parameters.
An optional secondary navigation link (e.g., "Back to Group Details") may use browser history (back) behavior; no additional interface calls are made by this interaction.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the groupSessionId URL parameter. When the page loads, it attempts to read groupSessionId from the query string (for example, group_registration.html?groupSessionId=...). If groupSessionId is present, the page enters a 'loading-context' state and calls getGroupRegistrationContext with that value. On success, the page transitions to a 'ready' state where GroupProgram and GroupSession data, defaultNumParticipants, and allowedPaymentOptions become available to downstream components (summary display and form). If the call fails or groupSessionId is missing/invalid, the page enters an 'error-context' state where the form remains disabled or hidden. During form submission, a 'submitting' state is set—preventing further edits to critical fields—until submitGroupRegistration returns. A successful submission moves the page into a 'confirmation' state, where the returned GroupRegistration object is stored in memory for this view and used to render confirmation details; URL parameters do not change during this step. If submission fails (success=false), the state returns to 'ready-with-errors', preserving all form data for resubmission. If the user or navigation changes the groupSessionId in the URL (for example, by navigating from a different session link), a full page load or a URL change listener should detect the new groupSessionId and repeat the getGroupRegistrationContext flow, effectively resetting the page to the 'loading-context' state for the new session.

components.functionality: Reads the groupSessionId from the URL query parameters on load, calls getGroupRegistrationContext with that ID, and exposes the resulting GroupProgram, GroupSession, defaultNumParticipants, allowedPaymentOptions, and loading/error statuses to other components.

Displays a read-only summary of the selected group program and session using data from registration-context-loader, including program title, topic, format, location (if available), facilitators (if available), start_date, end_date, weekday, start_time, end_time, and any capacity and registered_count values.

Collects participant_first_name, participant_last_name, participant_email, num_participants, and payment_option for the selected group session. Initializes num_participants from defaultNumParticipants and constrains it based on remaining capacity when available. Validates required fields and email format, and on submit calls submitGroupRegistration with the current form values and the groupSessionId from the URL.

Renders selectable payment options based on the allowedPaymentOptions array from getGroupRegistrationContext. Ensures that only one paymentOption code can be selected and passes the selected value back to group-registration-form for inclusion in the submitGroupRegistration call.

Shows dynamic status messages for loading, context errors, validation errors, submission progress, and submission results. When submitGroupRegistration returns, it displays the message and differentiates between success and error states.

After a successful submitGroupRegistration call, displays confirmation details using the returned GroupRegistration object (including participant name, email, num_participants, payment_option, and status) and hides or disables the editable form fields.

Provides navigation actions after registration. Includes a link to index.html for returning to the home page and optionally a control to return to the previous page via browser history. These links are primarily shown in the confirmation state but may also be available in error states.

layout.grid_system: Implicit responsive grid, likely a 12-column layout with a centered max-width content container (approx. 1080–1200px) and full-bleed hero image band.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top‑to‑bottom, single‑column layout that mirrors the mockup. Above, the global header and hero band remain consistent with the rest of the site: logo left, nav right in the header, then a full-width, soft photographic hero with a centered serif page title 'Group Registration'. Below the hero, all components sit within one centered content column (approx. 60–70% of viewport width, constrained to ~1080–1200px max), stacked in a logical sequence: loader/summary at the top, then status, form and payment selection, and finally confirmation and post‑registration links. No multi-column layouts; all interactive elements are arranged vertically to minimize cognitive load.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top‑to‑bottom, single‑column layout that mirrors the mockup. Above, the global header and hero band remain consistent with the rest of the site: logo left, nav right in the header, then a full-width, soft photographic hero with a centered serif page title 'Group Registration'. Below the hero, all components sit within one centered content column (approx. 60–70% of viewport width, constrained to ~1080–1200px max), stacked in a logical sequence: loader/summary at the top, then status, form and payment selection, and finally confirmation and post‑registration links. No multi-column layouts; all interactive elements are arranged vertically to minimize cognitive load.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header uses a modest but airy vertical height with comfortable padding (~16–24px). The hero occupies a noticeable band (roughly 30–40% of the initial viewport height) with generous top/bottom padding, keeping the 'Group Registration' title as the main visual anchor. There is a clear vertical gap (~48–64px) between the hero and the main content column, echoing the mockup’s spacious start to the content area. Each major block (session summary, form, confirmation) has substantial vertical margins between them (~32–48px), and internal padding of ~16–24px on all sides. Horizontally, the content column is centered, leaving calm whitespace on either side, especially on larger screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally low-density and breathable. Text lines use comfortable line heights, and form fields are stacked with clear vertical separation. No tight clustering of controls: field groups are separated by ~16–24px, major subsections by ~24–32px. Lists (such as confirmation details or navigation links) are spaced so each line has clear separation. Labels are concise and left-aligned, with generous padding within panels, mirroring the minimal, calm, counseling-appropriate visual language from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows from the logo on the top left through the right-aligned navigation, then drops to the centered hero title 'Group Registration'. From there, it moves straight down into the main content column: first to the group session summary (context), then to any status messaging, then into the main registration form (participant details), followed by the payment option selector as a clearly delineated sub-section. Once a registration is submitted, attention is guided down to the confirmation panel and then to the post-registration navigation links. The pattern is a soft Z at the top (header/hero) that quickly resolves into a strong vertical reading path, consistent with the original mockup’s flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar site header and a full-width hero band with a softly blurred nature image and the centered serif title 'Group Registration', the page transitions into a calm, light green-tinted content area. A single, centered content column sits comfortably in the middle of the screen, occupying roughly two-thirds of the viewport width on desktop, framed by generous off‑white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, the context loader or a concise group session summary sets the stage, followed by a subtle status banner if needed. Beneath this, the main registration form flows vertically in one column: participant details first, then a clearly separated payment options section, all spaced with generous padding and soft separators. After submission, a confirmation panel appears in the same central column, reaffirming the registration in a calm, card-like block, followed by a small set of next-step navigation links. The entire layout feels light and unhurried, with ample vertical spacing between sections and no visual clutter, reflecting the minimalist, nature-inspired, and reassuring tone of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-context-loader occupies the topmost position within the main content column directly below the hero section’s bottom edge. It is centered horizontally within the column, matching the column width (about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop, constrained to the site’s max content width). When active, it appears as a low-profile horizontal area with a centered spinner or loading message, surrounded by ample vertical whitespace: ~48–64px of space above from the hero and ~24–32px below before the group-session-summary would appear. When loading, this component effectively takes the place of the session summary and form, so they either do not render or are visually pushed below it, keeping the user’s focus at this top center location. On smaller screens, it expands to full width with 16–24px horizontal padding from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content column; when active, it sits at the top of the column and temporarily takes precedence over the summary and form content, but it does not fix or float—it scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group-session-summary panel is the first substantive content block in the main column beneath the hero (or beneath the loader if it is still visible). It appears roughly 48–64px below the bottom of the hero band, occupying the full width of the central column. The panel itself has a lightly tinted background (a soft green or off‑white) with rounded corners and internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides, echoing the calm card feel from the mockup’s content area. Text inside is left-aligned, starting with a concise heading (e.g., session name or date) at the top, followed by 1–3 short lines of key details stacked vertically with comfortable line spacing. There is ~24–32px of vertical space below the panel before the next component (registration-status-banner), preserving the spacious rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the main column; it scrolls with the page and sets the contextual anchor for all subsequent registration components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-status-banner sits directly beneath the group-session-summary, aligned to the same central column width so its left and right edges line up cleanly with the summary and form. It spans the full width of that column but is visually slimmer than a full panel—more like a subtle horizontal stripe or slim card with 12–16px vertical padding. When present (e.g., showing an error, warning, or success message), it appears with ~16–24px of vertical space above and below it, so it doesn’t crowd either the session summary or the form. Its background color is slightly more pronounced than the content background to draw attention (e.g., a soft green for success, muted amber for warnings), but still gentle and consistent with the nature-inspired palette. Text is left-aligned, kept to one or two short lines, and does not wrap into dense blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky banner within the column; appears and disappears based on state but otherwise stays in the vertical flow between summary and form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group-registration-form is the dominant content block and sits immediately below the registration-status-banner (or directly under the group-session-summary if no banner is visible). It fills the same central column width, forming a tall, single-column card-like section with a light background and soft border or shadow. The top of the form begins with a short, left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Participant Information') separated from the summary/banner above by ~24–32px of vertical space. All fields are stacked vertically, one per row, with labels above the inputs. Each field group has about 16–24px of vertical spacing between it and the next, ensuring a calm, uncrowded feel. The form is visually organized into 2–3 sub-sections (e.g., participant details, contact info, additional notes), each separated by a slightly larger gap (~24–32px) and possibly a subtle subheading. The form’s bottom area, where the submission button lives, has extra padding (~24–32px) and clear spacing above the payment-option-selector section, so the user can visually parse the flow from details to payment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block form within the column; it grows vertically based on the number of fields and scrolls naturally. No fixed or floating elements within the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-option-selector appears as a distinct sub-section near the lower portion of the group-registration-form. It aligns fully with the form’s left and right edges, maintaining the same column width and internal padding. A small left-aligned subheading (e.g., 'Payment Options') sits at its top with ~24px of vertical space separating it from the preceding form fields. Below, payment options (e.g., 'Pay now', 'Bill later', 'Use insurance') are presented as a vertical stack of radio-style cards or simple rows, each with 12–16px vertical padding and 8–16px of spacing between options, avoiding any side-by-side choices to keep the layout simple and consistent with the single-column pattern. The section ends with a slight gap (~24px) before the final form submit button, creating a visual hierarchy: choose payment, then submit. On mobile, this section simply flows as another readable block in the linear stack, without any layout change beyond natural wrapping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an internal section of the form, flowing within the same scroll context; no special fixed or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation-panel appears in the same central column, directly below the area previously occupied by the form’s submit action. In a success state where the registration is confirmed, this panel either replaces the form content or appears immediately beneath it with a generous vertical gap (~32–48px) separating the two, depending on the interaction model. It reads as a prominent, calm card: a soft, pale green or off‑white background, rounded corners, and 24px internal padding. A concise, left-aligned success heading at the top is followed by short confirmation text and a simple, vertically stacked list of key registration details (e.g., group title, date/time, location), each detail line spaced by 8–12px. The card’s width matches the main column, maintaining alignment with the summary and form. There is ~24–32px of space below this panel before the post-registration-navigation links begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that appears conditional on successful registration; it scrolls naturally with the page. It occupies the same general vertical space previously used by the form, reinforcing continuity in the user’s visual flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-registration-navigation appears directly under the registration-confirmation-panel within the same content column, acting as a light, unobtrusive footer for this page’s main flow. It consists of a short label or is implied by context, followed by 2–4 links arranged vertically with 8–12px spacing between each (e.g., 'Return to Groups', 'View Client Portal', 'Go to Home'). The entire block sits within the same column width and is left-aligned, echoing the typography of other body text. There is a moderate amount of space (~24–32px) between this navigation block and the confirmation panel above, and additional bottom margin (~48–64px) before the end of the page content, allowing the layout to breathe out gently rather than ending abruptly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline navigation list at the bottom of the main content column; it scrolls with the rest of the page and does not stick or float.

name: Self-Assessments
title: Self-Assessments
description: Lists available mental health self-assessment tools, shows brief details and disclaimers, and lets users start an assessment that continues on the assessment detail page.

core_features: Fetch and display a list of available self-assessments using getAssessmentList.
Show key information for each assessment, including name, description, and estimated completion time, to help users choose.
Provide a page-level guidance/disclaimer about the informational nature of self-assessments.
Optionally allow basic client-side sorting or filtering of assessments (e.g., by name or estimated time) using URL parameters to represent state.
Allow users to start an assessment by navigating to assessment_detail.html with the appropriate assessmentCode URL parameter.
user_workflows:
1. User navigates to Self-Assessments via header or footer link.
   1.1. Browser loads assessments.html.
   1.2. Page reads any existing URL parameters (e.g., sort, maxMinutes) to determine initial state.
   1.3. Page invokes getAssessmentList with no parameters.
   1.4. When getAssessmentList resolves, the page-level data store is populated with the array of Assessment objects.
   1.5. The list of assessments is rendered according to the current sort/filter state derived from the URL.
   1.6. A general informational disclaimer about self-assessments is displayed at the top of the page.

2. User adjusts sorting or filtering of assessments.
   2.1. User interacts with sorting controls (e.g., chooses to sort by name or estimated_minutes) and/or quick filters (e.g., "shorter assessments").
   2.2. The interaction handler updates the current URL (e.g., assessments.html?sort=name&direction=asc or assessments.html?maxMinutes=10) using the History API, without reloading the page.
   2.3. The page re-applies the sort/filter logic to the already-loaded Assessment array.
   2.4. The list UI re-renders to reflect the new ordering or filtered subset.

3. User reviews an individual assessment’s details in the list.
   3.1. User scans the list; for each Assessment, they see its name, description, estimated_minutes (if present), and a per-assessment disclaimer if available in Assessment.disclaimer.
   3.2. User can expand/collapse additional information for an assessment if the component supports it (e.g., to view longer descriptions) without additional API calls.

4. User starts a specific assessment.
   4.1. Within a list item, the user clicks the "Start" action for a chosen assessment.
   4.2. The click handler reads the selected Assessment.code from the bound data.
   4.3. The page navigates to assessment_detail.html with assessmentCode set to the Assessment.code, e.g., assessment_detail.html?assessmentCode=<assessment.code>.
   4.4. The assessment_detail.html page (outside the scope of this design) handles loading questions and running the session based on that parameter.

5. User arrives with a pre-defined URL state (direct link).
   5.1. A user opens a shared link like assessments.html?sort=estimated_minutes&direction=asc.
   5.2. On page load, the state logic parses sort and direction from the URL.
   5.3. getAssessmentList is called as usual; when data is loaded, the initial render applies the specified sort.
   5.4. UI controls (e.g., dropdowns) reflect the state derived from the URL so the user sees which sort/filter is active.
interactions:
Page load:
- Event: window.onload / component mount.
- Action: Parse URL parameters (sort, direction, maxMinutes, view) and derive initial state.
- Action: Call getAssessmentList().
- Action: Store the resulting Assessment array in page state.
- Action: Apply initial sort/filter and render the list and disclaimers.

Sorting control interaction:
- Component: assessments-sort-controls.
- Event: onChangeSort (e.g., user selects a sort field from a dropdown).
- Action: Update in-memory sort state (field and direction).
- Action: Update URL query string to reflect the new sort and direction without page reload.
- Action: Re-sort the current Assessment array client-side and trigger a re-render of the list.

Time-based filter interaction (optional):
- Component: assessments-filter-controls.
- Event: onChangeMaxMinutes (e.g., user selects a maximum desired estimated time).
- Action: Update in-memory filter state (maxMinutes).
- Action: Update URL query string (e.g., add or change maxMinutes parameter).
- Action: Filter the Assessment array where estimated_minutes is defined and less than or equal to maxMinutes; re-render the list.

Assessment list item interaction:
- Component: assessment-list-item.
- Event: onToggleDetails (optional; e.g., user clicks "More details").
- Action: Toggle an internal expanded/collapsed state for that item (keyed by assessment.id).
- Action: When expanded, show full Assessment.description and any Assessment.disclaimer text.

Start assessment interaction:
- Component: assessment-list-item.
- Event: onClickStart.
- Precondition: The component has the selected Assessment object as props/bound data.
- Action: Read assessment.code from the bound Assessment.
