event_handlers: onChangeTopic: When the user chooses a topic, update the topic parameter in the URL, reset offset to 0, optionally set limit if needed, and notify the controller to call getArticlesByTopic with the new topic.
onClearTopic: If the user clears the topic filter, remove the topic parameter from the URL and hide or reset the topic-articles-section.

components.functionality: Displays a list of articles filtered by the current topic, with pagination support.
event_handlers: onInitLoadByTopic: On page load, if topic exists in the URL, request getArticlesByTopic(topic, limit, offset) via the controller.
onTopicChanged: When topic changes (detected via URL change), request getArticlesByTopic with the updated topic and reset offset as needed.
onChangePage: When pagination-controls indicate a new offset or limit, update the URL and trigger getArticlesByTopic with the new paging values.
onRetryLoadByTopic: Allow user-initiated retries of getArticlesByTopic on error.
onToggleSave: For each article item, delegate save/unsave actions to article-save-toggle.

components.functionality: Reusable list component that renders an array of Article items in either the recommended or topic-based context, including saved-state markers and action controls.
event_handlers: onRenderItems: For each Article in the provided array, create an article-list-item instance with the correct saved/unsaved state based on ReadingList.article_ids (if available).
onClickArticle: When an article item is clicked, navigate to the article detail page using its id or slug without calling additional interfaces from this page.
onToggleSaveRequested: Bubble up save/unsave requests from article-list-item to the controller or directly to article-save-toggle.

components.functionality: Represents a single article entry, showing its title, summary excerpt, and associated actions like save/unsave and "Read" navigation.
event_handlers: onClickOpenArticle: Navigate to a full article view (for example, article.html with an id or slug parameter derived from the Article record).
onClickSaveToggle: Invoke article-save-toggle with the current Article.id and current saved state; update local visual state based on responses propagated from the controller.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic for toggling an article's saved state in the user's reading list by calling the appropriate interface and updating in-page ReadingList state.
event_handlers: onClickSave: If the current article is not in the in-memory ReadingList.article_ids, call saveArticleToReadingList(articleId). On success, forward the returned ReadingList to the controller via onReadingListUpdated.

onClickUnsave: If the current article is in the in-memory ReadingList.article_ids, call removeArticleFromReadingList(articleId). On success, forward the returned ReadingList to the controller via onReadingListUpdated.
onUpdateVisualState: After onReadingListUpdated, update the button label/state (e.g., from "Save" to "Saved" or vice versa) based on whether the Article.id is in ReadingList.article_ids.
onError: If saveArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList fails, revert any optimistic UI changes and optionally display non-intrusive error messaging (without changing URL parameters).

components.functionality: Provides next/previous and page-size controls for the topic-articles-section, mapping user actions to limit and offset URL parameters.
event_handlers: onClickNextPage: Calculate a new offset based on current offset and limit, update the offset parameter in the URL, and trigger the controller to call getArticlesByTopic with the new offset.
onClickPreviousPage: Decrease the offset (not below 0), update the offset parameter in the URL, and trigger getArticlesByTopic with the new offset.
onChangePageSize: When the user selects a different page size, update the limit parameter, reset offset to 0, write both to the URL, and trigger getArticlesByTopic with the new values.

components.functionality: Provides a clear navigation entry point from the resources page to reading_list.html.
event_handlers: onClickOpenReadingList: Navigate the browser to reading_list.html. Optionally, if the implementation chooses to show a count badge, use the length of ReadingList.article_ids (from the most recent save/remove operation) as the displayed count, without making additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit responsive grid, likely a 12-column layout with a centered max-width content container (approx. 1080–1200px) and full-bleed hero image band.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a single-column, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the Pricing mockup: a left-logo/right-nav header at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band with a centered page title ('Resources & Articles'). Below the hero, all resource-related content is presented in a vertically stacked central column: first a compact 'Recommended Articles' section, then a larger 'Browse by Topic' section that begins with filters and continues into a scrollable article list with pagination. All components are aligned within the same central content column, creating a calm, linear reading experience.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a single-column, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the Pricing mockup: a left-logo/right-nav header at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band with a centered page title ('Resources & Articles'). Below the hero, all resource-related content is presented in a vertically stacked central column: first a compact 'Recommended Articles' section, then a larger 'Browse by Topic' section that begins with filters and continues into a scrollable article list with pagination. All components are aligned within the same central content column, creating a calm, linear reading experience.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is a modest-height horizontal bar with generous vertical padding (around 16–24px) and standard horizontal margins. The hero band is full-bleed, occupying roughly 30–40% of the initial viewport height, visually anchoring the page. The main content sits inside a centered container up to about 1080–1200px wide, with the main column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport on desktop and comfortable side gutters. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → recommended → topic section → footer) is in the 32–64px range, with 16–24px internal padding for each content block and list. Lists and rows use 8–16px spacing between items to keep things airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density is intentionally low, with short text labels, clear section headings, and vertically separated lists. Each article entry has room to breathe, avoiding dense card grids in favor of simple stacked rows. Filters are presented in a light, unobtrusive strip rather than a heavy filter panel. Line heights are generous, and only a moderate number of articles are shown per page (e.g., 5–10) to maintain a calm, approachable feel befitting a counseling site.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves first from the logo at the top-left across the right-aligned navigation, where the 'Resources & Articles' link and 'Reading List' link sit alongside other primary pages. The centered hero title then creates a strong focal point and anchors the user in the page context. From there, the visual path moves straight down into the left-aligned 'Recommended for You' heading and its short list, then continues down into the 'Browse by Topic' heading, the horizontal band of filter controls, and finally down through the vertical column of topic-based articles and pagination. The overall flow is a gentle Z (header) into a vertical reading path, with minimal competing visual elements.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a familiar, airy header: the counseling practice logo on the top-left and a simple right-aligned navigation including 'Home', 'About', 'Services', 'Pricing', 'Resources & Articles', 'Contact', 'Client Portal', and a distinct 'Reading List' link. Directly beneath, a full-width hero band with a soft, blurred nature photograph stretches edge-to-edge, with the serif page title 'Resources & Articles' centered both horizontally and vertically in the band. Below the hero, the main content sits within a centered, light green-tinted column that occupies about two-thirds of the page width on desktop, framed by generous whitespace on both sides. The first block is a 'Recommended Articles' section: a compact heading and a short list of 3–5 items, each a simple, full-width row with title and short meta text, lightly separated. After a comfortable vertical gap, the second block—'Browse by Topic'—introduces a subtle horizontal strip of filter controls at the top, followed by a vertically stacked list of articles filtered by topic. Each article row feels like a soft, unobtrusive card with a clear save icon or button aligned to the right. At the very bottom of this section, centered pagination controls allow gentle navigation between pages of articles. Throughout, the layout remains clean, linear, and breathable, with no multi-column complexity, maintaining the same calm, reassuring rhythm as the original Pricing mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual orchestration layer that spans the entire page structure. Conceptually, it wraps the content starting just below the hero band and extending to the bottom of the main content area. It governs which articles appear in the 'Recommended Articles' and 'Topic Articles' lists and synchronizes the state of filter controls, save toggles, and pagination. Although it has no visible footprint, it should be treated as enveloping the main column that holds the recommended-articles-section and topic-articles-section, ensuring both sections stay within the same centered max-width container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; participates only in data flow and state management without affecting layout directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended articles section sits at the top of the main content column, starting approximately 48px below the bottom edge of the hero band. It is left-aligned within a centered content container that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at roughly 1080–1200px. The section begins with a concise heading (e.g., 'Recommended for You') aligned to the left, with about 16–24px of padding above and below. Directly beneath this heading, the article-list for recommendations is presented as a vertical stack of 3–5 items, each taking the full width of the internal column. There is approximately 16px vertical spacing between the section heading and the first article item, and 12–16px spacing between subsequent items, maintaining a light, airy feel. The entire section is visually wrapped in a subtle, light-tinted background that blends with the page’s light green tone, with around 24px internal padding on all sides and 32–40px margin below before the next section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the normal document flow, stretching to fit the width of the central content column and pushing subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic filter controls sit at the top of the topic-articles-section, immediately below that section’s heading. Within the same centered column, they form a horizontal band that spans nearly the full width of the content column (around 90–100%), with 16–24px of vertical padding and subtle separation from the background (e.g., a very light tint or bottom border). Controls such as dropdowns, pill buttons, or a search field are left-aligned, laid out side by side with 8–16px horizontal gaps. On wider screens, filters present as a compact, single-row grouping; on smaller screens they wrap to multiple rows while preserving at least 8px vertical spacing between lines. The entire filter band has about 16px of space below it before the first topic article item begins, and roughly 16px above it separating it from the section heading. It feels visually light and does not overpower the headings or article rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the topic-articles-section, expanding vertically as needed when controls wrap on smaller screens, but always constrained to the width of the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended articles section sits at the top of the main content column, starting approximately 48px below the bottom edge of the hero band. It is left-aligned within a centered content container that spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at roughly 1080–1200px. The section begins with a concise heading (e.g., 'Recommended for You') aligned to the left, with about 16–24px of padding above and below. Directly beneath this heading, the article-list for recommendations is presented as a vertical stack of 3–5 items, each taking the full width of the internal column. There is approximately 16px vertical spacing between the section heading and the first article item, and 12–16px spacing between subsequent items, maintaining a light, airy feel. The entire section is visually wrapped in a subtle, light-tinted background that blends with the page’s light green tone, with around 24px internal padding on all sides and 32–40px margin below before the next section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the normal document flow, stretching to fit the width of the central content column and pushing subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-articles-section appears directly below the recommended-articles-section, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 40–48px to preserve the calm pacing. It uses the same centered content container and column width, aligning flush with the recommended section above. At the top of this block is a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Browse by Topic'), followed by 16px of vertical space, then the topic-filter-controls band. Below the filter controls, the main article-list for topics fills the remaining vertical space as a stacked list of rows, each row spanning the full width of the column with consistent left alignment. The entire section may sit on the same light green-tinted background as the page or be lightly differentiated with a subtle panel feel, but the boundaries remain soft. The section has approximately 24px internal padding and 48–64px margin at the bottom before transitioning to any footer content. Pagination-controls are placed centered at the bottom of this section, separated from the last article row by around 24–32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primary content section in normal flow, growing vertically with the number of article rows, with the pagination-controls anchored to the bottom of the section’s content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article-list acts as a vertical stack container for article-list-item components within its parent section. In both the recommended-articles-section and the topic-articles-section, the list spans 100% of the internal content column width, starting flush left with section text. Items are stacked top-to-bottom with consistent gaps of 12–16px between them, ensuring comfortable breathing room. The list itself does not introduce strong box boundaries; instead, it relies on internal item styling and consistent spacing to define structure. In the recommended section, the list is relatively short (3–5 items) and therefore occupies a modest vertical slice, while in the topic section it may extend longer (up to a full viewport height or more), with pagination controlling the total. On smaller screens, the list simply narrows to full-viewport width with margins, preserving the single-column vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A flexible, vertical flow container that expands in height based on the number of article-list-item children, always constrained to the width of its parent section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article-list-item is a horizontally oriented row within the article-list, extending almost the full width of the content column (e.g., 100% width with 16–24px internal padding). The main text content (title, short description, and any metadata like reading time) is left-aligned and occupies roughly 80–90% of the available row width, while the article-save-toggle sits to the right. Vertically, each item is tall enough to allow 8–12px padding above and below the text block inside, so the row feels like a soft card rather than a tight line. There is a clear 12–16px gap below each item before the next one begins. Background treatment is subtle—a very light tint or a soft border radius—to keep the page clean and aligned with the minimalist aesthetic. On narrower screens, the save toggle may stack below or align under the text while staying right-aligned, but the row remains a single-column block with a clear top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level items in the vertical list; they maintain a consistent height based on content and participate in normal document flow, with internal alignment managing the position of the save toggle.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-save-toggle is positioned inside each article-list-item, aligned to the far right of the row. On desktop, it sits horizontally in line with the article title or just below it, occupying roughly 10–15% of the row width but visually appearing as a compact icon button or small text+icon button. There is at least 8–12px of padding between the toggle and the right edge of the article row to keep it from feeling cramped. Vertically, it is centered relative to the item’s text block so that the row feels balanced. On smaller screens, if horizontal space is limited, the toggle may drop to a second line but remains right-aligned within the item’s internal padding box, separated from the main text by about 8–12px of vertical space. The toggle is visually distinct enough to be easily found but small and light enough to avoid overpowering the calm, content-first layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each article-list-item, anchored to the right side; its position may wrap to a second line on narrow viewports while maintaining right alignment and internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of the topic-articles-section, centered horizontally within the main content column. They are separated from the last article-list-item by a comfortable vertical space of about 24–32px and from any section bottom edge by another 16–24px, so they do not feel cramped. The pagination elements (previous/next buttons and page numbers) form a compact horizontal group that occupies no more than 30–40% of the column width, leaving generous whitespace on either side. The controls are visually understated, using light outlines or minimal styling to remain consistent with the calm design language. On smaller screens, if needed, page numbers can wrap to a second line while the entire group remains centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A centered horizontal control group fixed to the bottom of the topic-articles-section content; it does not stick on scroll and appears only after the article list items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-nav-link is placed in the top navigation bar, aligned to the right alongside other navigation items. It sits in the same horizontal row as 'Home', 'About', 'Services', 'Pricing', 'Resources & Articles', 'Contact', and 'Client Portal'. The nav items are right-aligned inside the header’s max-width container, with the reading list link positioned near the end of the list—either immediately after 'Resources & Articles' or adjacent to the client-facing items—so it is easy to discover as a related destination. Each nav link has 16–24px horizontal padding, and the entire nav row has about 16–24px vertical padding, mirroring the airy header from the mockup. The reading list link is styled consistently with other nav links but can have a subtle distinguishing cue (like a slightly different weight) while maintaining the overall calm aesthetic. On mobile, it appears within the collapsed nav or stacked list, keeping its relative order near 'Resources & Articles'.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline navigation element within the primary header bar; it inherits any sticky or fixed behavior of the header but does not have independent scrolling behavior.

name: Reading List
title: Reading List
description: View and manage saved mental health articles and resources. Users can review items they saved earlier, open them in the resources view, remove items they no longer need, and see a reassurance about the privacy of their list.

core_features: Load the current user's saved reading list using getSavedReadingList and display all associated Article records.
Display a privacy reassurance text using the privacyNote returned by getSavedReadingList, clearly indicating that the reading list is private and for personal reference.
Allow users to open any saved article/resource in a new view or tab by navigating to resources.html with an article identifier in the URL parameters.
Allow users to remove individual articles from their reading list by invoking removeArticleFromReadingList and updating the displayed list based on the returned ReadingList.
Provide an optional topic-based filter of the reading list that is driven by a URL parameter (e.g., topic), so users can quickly view only items matching a specific topic.
Handle an optional highlightArticleId URL parameter to emphasize or scroll to a particular saved article, typically when a user has just added it from another page.
Show an empty-state message when the reading list contains no articles and provide a navigation action that takes users to resources.html to find more articles.
user_workflows: View saved reading list: User opens reading_list.html (optionally with URL parameters); onPageLoad triggers getSavedReadingList; the page displays privacyNote, then lists all articles from the response, optionally filtered by the topic URL parameter; if highlightArticleId is present, the corresponding article is emphasized or scrolled into view.

Open a saved article in a new tab: User locates an article in the list; user clicks the article title or a dedicated 'Open' action; the browser navigates to resources.html with an articleId URL parameter (for example, resources.html?articleId={Article.id}&source=reading_list), opening in a new tab or window according to the link target; the reading_list.html page remains unchanged.

Remove an article from the reading list: User finds an article in the list; user clicks a 'Remove from reading list' action associated with that article; onClickRemove calls removeArticleFromReadingList with articleId; onRemoveSuccess updates the in-page ReadingList state using the returned readingList (and optionally re-renders any topic filter options); if the list becomes empty, the empty-state panel is shown along with a link to resources.html.

Filter reading list by topic via URL: User visits reading_list.html with a topic URL parameter (for example, reading_list.html?topic={topicTag}); onPageLoad, getSavedReadingList is called; the list of articles is then filtered client-side to include only those whose topics include the topic parameter; the active filter is shown in the filter controls; if the user clears or changes the filter, the component updates the URL parameters (e.g., replaces topic or removes it) and re-applies the filtering logic without recalling the API.

Navigate to discover more resources: User is on reading_list.html (whether the list is empty or not); user clicks a 'Find more articles' link or button; onClickFindMore opens resources.html (optionally passing along a topic parameter if the user has a topic filter active on the reading list), so the user can browse or save additional articles.
interactions: Page load: When reading_list.html loads, onPageLoad calls getSavedReadingList with no parameters; the response populates the ReadingList, the list of Article items, and the privacyNote; then the list view applies any topic and highlightArticleId filtering or emphasis based on the current URL parameters.

Click article title or open action: For each article, clicking the title or an 'Open' control triggers onClickOpenArticle, which navigates to resources.html with articleId (and optionally source=reading_list) in the query string, set to open in a new tab or window.

Click 'Remove' on an article: For each article, clicking a remove control triggers onClickRemove; this invokes removeArticleFromReadingList(articleId); on success, the component updates the local ReadingList state using the returned readingList and re-renders the article list; on failure, an error message is displayed inline or near the affected item.

Click 'Find more articles': Clicking a 'Find more articles' action triggers onClickFindMore; this navigates the browser to resources.html, optionally including the current topic filter as a URL parameter so that the resources page can pre-filter content.

Change topic filter: Interacting with the topic filter control triggers onFilterChange; this updates the reading_list.html URL (e.g., replaces or removes the topic parameter) via a URL update method and then re-applies client-side filtering of the already loaded articles without additional API calls.

Clear all filters: Clicking a 'Clear filter' action triggers onClearFilters; this removes the topic parameter from the URL; the list view then shows all articles from the current getSavedReadingList response.

Scroll or highlight a specific article: If the URL contains highlightArticleId when the page renders or when the URL is updated, the list component uses onHighlightChange to locate the matching Article item in the rendered list and apply visual emphasis or scroll it into view.
page_functionality.state_logic: The reading_list.html page uses URL query parameters to control view state while all persisted reading list data comes from getSavedReadingList. Supported parameters include: (1) topic: a string representing a topic tag. After getSavedReadingList returns the full articles array, the client filters this array to only display articles whose topics include the topic value. Changing this filter updates the topic parameter in the URL and re-renders the list; the underlying ReadingList from the API is not modified by this parameter. (2) highlightArticleId: an Article.id value. After the initial list render, the component checks for a matching article and, if present, scrolls to and/or visually emphasizes that article. This parameter does not cause an additional API call; it only affects which item is highlighted. (3) Additional non-persistent view parameters such as source may be appended when navigating from other pages but are treated as read-only context (for example, to decide whether to show a one-time message); they do not change API calls or the underlying ReadingList. The page always initializes by calling getSavedReadingList without URL-derived parameters; all URL parameters only influence client-side filtering, highlighting, and navigation behaviors. After removeArticleFromReadingList modifies the reading list, the updated ReadingList returned by that API replaces the current in-memory ReadingList and the same URL-driven filtering and highlighting logic is re-applied.

components.functionality: Fetches the current user's ReadingList, associated Article records, and privacyNote on page load, and provides a method to refresh this data after list updates.
Displays the privacy reassurance text returned as privacyNote from getSavedReadingList so users understand that their reading list is private and for personal reference.

Reads the topic URL parameter on initialization and exposes controls to change or clear the topic filter. Updates the URL parameters when the filter changes and notifies the list component to re-apply client-side filtering on the Article collection.

Renders the collection of saved articles from the ReadingList, after applying topic and highlightArticleId logic derived from URL parameters. For each Article, shows key fields (such as title and summary) and attaches open/remove actions. Reacts to ReadingList updates from removeArticleFromReadingList by re-rendering the filtered list.

Provides per-article controls within the list: opening the article in resources.html with the appropriate articleId parameter, and removing the article from the reading list via removeArticleFromReadingList. Handles the success and error responses to update the parent list view state or surface error feedback.

Detects when the ReadingList has no article_ids or when the filtered article set is empty, and displays an appropriate guidance message along with a navigation action to resources.html to help users discover and save new articles.

Provides navigation actions from the reading list to resources.html. When invoked (for example, via a 'Find more articles' link), constructs a URL to resources.html, optionally including the current topic filter in the query string so that the resources page can present relevant content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit responsive grid, likely a 12-column layout with a centered max-width content container (approx. 1080–1200px) and full-bleed hero image band.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow: global header at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band with the page title 'Reading List' centered. Below the hero, a single, centered content column holds the reading list experience in this order: privacy note, filters, the reading list (or empty state), and finally low-key navigation links at the bottom. All components are arranged in one vertical stack with left-aligned text inside the central column, echoing the mockup’s simple, calm structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow: global header at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band with the page title 'Reading List' centered. Below the hero, a single, centered content column holds the reading list experience in this order: privacy note, filters, the reading list (or empty state), and finally low-key navigation links at the bottom. All components are arranged in one vertical stack with left-aligned text inside the central column, echoing the mockup’s simple, calm structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, centered with soft whitespace on both sides. The hero band takes a visually prominent slice near the top, similar in height to the Pricing hero. Within the content column, each component has clear vertical separation—typically 24–40px between related sections and 40–64px before major shifts (e.g., between hero and the first text block, and before the footer-like navigation links). The reading list area itself (loader + list/empty state) occupies the majority of the vertical space, with filters and privacy note using modest height.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally low-density: short text blocks, generous line height, and comfortable vertical spacing between list items and controls. Filters are kept lightweight (a small number of controls in a single row) and list items breathe with internal padding in the 16–24px range. No dense tables or multi-column grids are used; each list item reads as a calm, lightly framed card-like row that’s easy to scan, matching the counseling context’s need for clarity and low cognitive load.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the header logo and navigation, then is pulled down to the centered 'Reading List' hero title. From there, attention moves into the content column at the left, first encountering the reassuring privacy note, then naturally sliding down to the filters, and finally settling into a vertical scanning pattern down the list items. Item actions are aligned to the right within each row, creating a subtle left-to-right micro-flow inside each item (title/description on the left; actions on the right). If the list is empty, the empty state panel becomes the focal point in the center of the column, then the eye gently exits through the understated navigation links near the bottom.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s consistent header, a full-bleed hero band with a soft, blurred nature photograph stretches across the screen. In the center of this band, the page title 'Reading List' sits in a calm, serif type, clearly anchoring the user in the current section. The main content begins a comfortable distance below the hero on a very light green-tinted background, forming a single, centered column that occupies about two-thirds of the page width. At the top of this column, a short, left-aligned privacy note in a smaller, muted type reassures users that their saved articles are private. A bit further down, the filter controls form a tidy horizontal strip—still left-aligned within the column but with enough whitespace above and below to feel unpressured. The reading list appears beneath that as a vertical stack of soft, card-like items; each item stretches across most of the column width with generous internal padding and room between rows, keeping the content airy. Within each item, the text (title, source, brief description) anchors on the left while a compact row of item actions sits on the right, aligned vertically in the middle, forming a gentle horizontal tension inside each row. When no items exist, a centered, lightly framed empty-state panel replaces the list, with a calm icon or illustration, a concise heading, and a short line of guidance. Finally, near the bottom of the column, a set of quiet navigation links (e.g., back to Resources or Home) sits with extra top and bottom spacing, acting like a soft footer for this page section while preserving the clean, breathable aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the same horizontal footprint as the reading list area, centered within the main content column below the filters. When active, it appears as a vertically centered element relative to the visible portion of the list region—typically a spinner or gentle progress indicator with a short label—positioned about one-third down from the top of the content column to avoid crowding the filters. It spans no more than 30–40% of the column width, sitting centrally with ample whitespace around it so the page still feels calm and uncluttered. In cases where list content is already present, the loader overlays a subtle translucent veil directly over the list portion only (not covering the header, hero, privacy note, or filters), keeping the user’s focus in the main content area while reinforcing that changes are in progress.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally and either replaces the list contents in their vertical slot or overlays them with a centered indicator within the list region; it does not affect the layout of the header, hero, privacy note, or filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy note is positioned at the very top of the main content column, starting roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero band. It is left-aligned within the column and constrained to the full width of the column on small screens, but effectively reads as a single, short paragraph spanning about 60–70% of the column width on desktop due to its limited character count. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space above it (from the hero) and about 24–32px of space below it before the filters begin, creating a soft, introductory buffer that feels reassuring rather than urgent. The text is relatively small and muted, so it does not compete visually with the hero or the list but still clearly precedes functional controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow of the content column, always appearing as the first textual element beneath the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls sit directly beneath the privacy note, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, occupying the full width of the main content column. On desktop, the filters are arranged in a single horizontal row aligned to the left: for example, a search field taking roughly 50–60% of the column width, followed by one or two compact dropdowns or pill filters to its right with 16–24px gaps between them. The entire filter bar has internal vertical padding of about 8–12px so the controls don’t feel cramped, but it remains visually lightweight—no heavy borders or large backgrounds—so that it doesn’t overpower the list it controls. There is a similar 24–32px gap below the filters before the first list item (or the empty state), reinforcing a clear separation between filtering and content while maintaining the calm rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal strip within the content column that wraps into multiple lines on smaller screens, but always stays directly above the list or empty state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list occupies the main body of the page, beginning about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the filter controls within the centered content column. It is a single vertical stack of items, each item spanning nearly the full width of the column (roughly 90–100% of the column width) with soft, rounded corners and a subtle background tint slightly stronger than the page’s light green to distinguish it from the canvas. Each item has generous internal padding—around 16–24px on all sides—and is separated from the next item by 16–24px of vertical space, allowing each resource to feel distinct and easy to scan. The item content is structured with the article title as a primary line near the top left, any meta details (source, category, date) beneath or just below it, and the item actions aligned to the right in the same vertical band as the title, creating a gentle left-to-right scan inside each row. As the user scrolls, the list extends downward in a straightforward vertical flow, with no side-by-side columns or dense grids, preserving the mockup’s calm, linear reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically as items are added; always anchored below the filters and above the navigation links, scrolling with the page as a standard content section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each list item, the item actions are positioned on the right side of the item’s content area, aligned horizontally in a single row and vertically centered relative to the main text block. They sit within the same padded container as the title and description, typically occupying the rightmost 20–30% of the item’s width while the left 70–80% is reserved for the article’s title and meta details. Each action (e.g., 'Open', 'Remove') is presented as a small text button or subtle icon-with-label with 8–16px spacing between actions, preserving the light, non-aggressive tone appropriate for a counseling site. On narrower screens, the actions naturally wrap beneath the text, still left-aligned relative to the content but separated by a small 8–12px vertical gap so the layout remains orderly and calm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within each list item’s container; on wider screens it stays right-aligned and horizontally grouped, while on smaller screens it reflows below the text content without leaving the item’s boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no saved items, the empty state panel appears in place of the list, starting in the same vertical slot where the list would normally begin—about 24–32px below the filters. The panel is centered within the main content column and occupies roughly 50–60% of the column width, giving it a balanced, contained presence with ample whitespace around it. Internally, it follows a simple vertical stack: a small, calm illustration or icon at the top, a concise heading directly below, and a short explanatory text line and call-to-action link or button beneath, all center-aligned inside the panel with 8–16px spacing between elements. There is extra vertical padding of about 24–32px inside the panel and around 32–40px of open space beneath it before the navigation links, so the user perceives it as a gentle, reassuring focal card rather than an error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the list in the same vertical position; it does not shift the privacy note, filters, or navigation links, and scrolls like a normal content block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links are positioned at the bottom of the main content column, always appearing below the list or the empty state with a generous 40–64px of vertical spacing above them to signal the end of the primary content area. They are left-aligned within the column and presented as a short row or stacked list of subtle text links (e.g., 'Back to Resources', 'Return to Home'), with 16–24px spacing between each link if shown in a row. The links use a lighter visual treatment—smaller size or softer color—than the main navigation in the header, so they read as quiet, contextual paths rather than primary calls to action. There is an additional 32–48px of padding below these links before the very bottom of the page, ensuring the layout ends on a soft, uncramped note consistent with the mockup’s spacious rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static part of the vertical flow; always anchored to the bottom of the content column and scrolling normally with the rest of the page.

name: Crisis Support
title: Crisis Support
description: Page for immediate crisis help: shows emergency instructions, local/national/specialized hotlines, and helps users choose local hotlines to use in their safety planning, with a direct link to the Safety Plan page.

core_features: Load crisis support content (intro text, emergency instructions, and categorized hotline lists) using the getCrisisSupportContent interface.
Display emergency instructions prominently so users immediately see what to do in an urgent crisis, based entirely on emergencyInstructions returned by getCrisisSupportContent.
Show categorized hotline lists (local, national, specialized) using the CrisisHotline arrays returned from getCrisisSupportContent, with actions to call each hotline.
Provide a tabbed view and optional filters so users can switch between local, national, and specialized hotlines and refine results using getCrisisHotlines.
Allow users to mark up to two local hotlines as "selected for safety planning", keeping the selected hotline IDs in URL parameters so they can be referenced and reviewed on this page.
Summarize selected local hotlines in a dedicated area to encourage users to note them down or use them when creating a safety plan.
Offer a clear "Create Safety Plan" button that navigates to safety_plan.html when clicked.
user_workflows:
1) View emergency instructions and local hotlines:
   - User opens crisis_support.html (with no or default URL parameters).
   - The page calls getCrisisSupportContent.
   - The emergencyInstructions text is displayed at the top of the page.
   - Below, the introText and default hotline view (e.g., all or local) are shown, using localHotlines, nationalHotlines, and specializedHotlines from the API response.

2) Switch between hotline categories (local, national, specialized):
   - User clicks a tab for a category (e.g., local, national, specialized) in the hotline tabs component.
   - The component updates the URL parameter hotlinesView to the selected category value.
   - Based on hotlinesView, the hotline list component chooses which array from getCrisisSupportContent to render (localHotlines, nationalHotlines, or specializedHotlines).
   - The list updates without calling any additional interfaces, since data is already loaded by getCrisisSupportContent.

3) Filter hotlines using getCrisisHotlines:
   - User opens the filter form, chooses a hotline type (matching the hotline_type enum) and optionally toggles a "local only" option.
   - User submits the filter form.
   - The filter component updates URL parameters filterHotlineType and filterIsLocal.
   - The filter component calls getCrisisHotlines with hotlineType set from filterHotlineType and isLocal set from filterIsLocal.
   - The hotline list component switches into "filtered view" mode and renders only the CrisisHotline entries returned by getCrisisHotlines.

4) Select up to two local hotlines for safety planning:
   - While viewing local hotlines (either from the default categorized lists or from filtered results), the user clicks an action such as "Select for Safety Plan" on a local hotline item.
   - The hotline list component checks the current selectedLocalHotlineIds value from the URL (parsed as an array of IDs).
   - If fewer than two hotline IDs are selected and the clicked hotline has is_local=true, its id is added to selectedLocalHotlineIds and the URL is updated accordingly.
   - If the hotline is already selected, clicking again removes its id from selectedLocalHotlineIds and updates the URL.
   - If the user tries to select more than two local hotlines, the component displays an inline validation message and does not modify selectedLocalHotlineIds.

5) Review selected hotlines and prepare for safety planning:
   - The selected-hotlines summary component reads selectedLocalHotlineIds from the URL and matches those IDs against the currently loaded CrisisHotline records (from getCrisisSupportContent and/or getCrisisHotlines results).
   - The component displays the corresponding hotlines, including their name and phone_number values.
   - The component encourages the user (via UI copy) to write down or remember these hotlines for their safety plan.
   - The user may adjust selections via the hotline list (which updates both the list state and the summary component through URL parameters).

6) Navigate to create a Safety Plan:
   - After identifying and optionally selecting local hotlines, the user clicks the "Create Safety Plan" button.
   - The page performs a simple navigation to safety_plan.html with no additional URL parameters (as specified by the outgoing_connections configuration).
   - Any preparation done on the Crisis Support page (e.g., which hotlines were selected) is meant for the user’s own reference and does not change how the navigation behaves.
interactions: Clicking category tabs (local/national/specialized) updates the hotlinesView URL parameter and switches which CrisisHotline list is displayed.

Submitting the hotline filter form triggers getCrisisHotlines with hotlineType and isLocal values from user input, updates filterHotlineType and filterIsLocal URL parameters, and refreshes the hotline list with the filtered results.
Clicking a hotline's "Call" action initiates a telephone call using the hotline's phone_number value via a tel: link, without altering URL state.
Clicking "Select for Safety Plan" on a local hotline adds or removes that hotline's id from the selectedLocalHotlineIds URL parameter (enforcing a maximum of two IDs).

Clicking a "Clear filters" action in the filter form removes filterHotlineType and filterIsLocal from the URL, and the hotline list reverts to using the categorized lists from getCrisisSupportContent according to hotlinesView.
Clicking the "Create Safety Plan" button navigates the browser to safety_plan.html.

Refreshing the page or sharing the URL preserves the current hotline category (hotlinesView), filter selections (filterHotlineType, filterIsLocal), and selected local hotlines (selectedLocalHotlineIds) because they are encoded in URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters control which hotlines are shown and which local hotlines are marked for safety planning. The following parameters are used:

- hotlinesView: Determines which category tab is active and which set of hotlines is rendered from getCrisisSupportContent. Allowed values correspond to category identifiers such as 'local', 'national', 'specialized', and optionally 'all' or 'filtered'. If hotlinesView is missing or invalid, a default category is chosen (e.g., local or all).

- filterHotlineType: When present, indicates that the hotline list is in filtered mode. Its value maps directly to the hotline_type enum expected by getCrisisHotlines. When filterHotlineType is set, the hotline list will use the result of getCrisisHotlines instead of the pre-loaded categorized arrays from getCrisisSupportContent.

- filterIsLocal: When present, indicates whether the filter should restrict results to local hotlines. It is parsed as a boolean (e.g., 'true' or 'false') and is passed as the isLocal parameter to getCrisisHotlines. If filterIsLocal is missing, the isLocal argument is left undefined when calling getCrisisHotlines.

- selectedLocalHotlineIds: A comma-separated list of CrisisHotline.id values representing local hotlines that the user has marked for safety planning. This parameter is read when rendering the hotline list and the selected-hotlines summary. On each selection or deselection, the component updates selectedLocalHotlineIds in the URL. The component also enforces that at most two IDs are retained in this list at any time.

- Additional UI-related state such as whether the emergency instructions are expanded or collapsed may be managed in component memory without URL parameters, since it does not affect data loading or cross-session behavior.

On initial load, the page always calls getCrisisSupportContent to retrieve introText, emergencyInstructions, and categorized hotline arrays. If filterHotlineType is present, the page also calls getCrisisHotlines with hotlineType set to filterHotlineType and isLocal set according to filterIsLocal. Changing hotlinesView or selectedLocalHotlineIds does not trigger new interface calls, as they only affect which already-loaded records are displayed and which are marked as selected. Changing filterHotlineType or filterIsLocal triggers a new getCrisisHotlines call and updates the displayed list.

components.functionality: Displays the static page title text for the Crisis Support page so users can confirm they are in the immediate help section.

On page load, calls getCrisisSupportContent to obtain introText, emergencyInstructions, and categorized hotline lists, and provides this data to child components. It also coordinates URL parameter parsing (hotlinesView, filterHotlineType, filterIsLocal, selectedLocalHotlineIds) and passes derived state down.

Shows the emergencyInstructions content prominently at the top of the page, ensuring users see clear guidance on what to do in an immediate crisis. Optionally allows expanding/collapsing if the text is long, but the core content comes entirely from the interface.
Displays the introText from getCrisisSupportContent, giving a concise explanation of what users can do on this page (e.g., find crisis numbers, choose hotlines for safety planning).

Renders tabs for switching between local, national, and specialized hotlines. The active tab is determined by the hotlinesView URL parameter, and clicking a tab updates hotlinesView and triggers the hotline list to show the corresponding category.

Allows users to refine the hotline list by selecting a hotline type and whether to show only local hotlines. On submission, it updates filterHotlineType and filterIsLocal URL parameters and calls getCrisisHotlines with those values. It also provides a clear-filters action to remove these parameters and revert to the categorized lists from getCrisisSupportContent.

Displays a list of CrisisHotline entries depending on the current state: either categorized lists from getCrisisSupportContent (localHotlines, nationalHotlines, specializedHotlines) according to hotlinesView, or the filtered results returned by getCrisisHotlines when filterHotlineType is set. Each hotline row includes key fields and actions to initiate a call and to toggle selection for safety planning (for local hotlines). The component reads and updates selectedLocalHotlineIds from the URL, enforcing a maximum of two selected local hotlines.

Reads selectedLocalHotlineIds from the URL, finds matching local CrisisHotline records from the data already loaded (either from getCrisisSupportContent.localHotlines or from getCrisisHotlines results), and shows a concise summary of up to two selected hotlines. Encourages the user to note these hotlines and consider including them when creating a safety plan.

Displays a primary action button labeled for creating or viewing a safety plan. When clicked, it navigates directly to safety_plan.html with no additional URL parameters, fulfilling the outgoing navigation requirement.

layout.grid_system: Implicit responsive grid, likely a 12-column layout with a centered max-width content container (approx. 1080–1200px) and full-bleed hero image band.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom single-column layout. The global header and nav remain at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band with the 'Crisis Support' page title centered in it. Below the hero, the main crisis content is contained in a centered column. Inside this column, components are stacked in a calm, logical order: emergency instructions first, then a brief intro/explanation, followed by hotline browsing controls (tabs, filters), then the hotline list, and finally a summary of selected hotlines and a clear call-to-action leading to the Safety Plan page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom single-column layout. The global header and nav remain at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band with the 'Crisis Support' page title centered in it. Below the hero, the main crisis content is contained in a centered column. Inside this column, components are stacked in a calm, logical order: emergency instructions first, then a brief intro/explanation, followed by hotline browsing controls (tabs, filters), then the hotline list, and finally a summary of selected hotlines and a clear call-to-action leading to the Safety Plan page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a full-bleed hero band occupying roughly 30–40% of the initial viewport height, with the title centered. Below it, a main content container sits centered on the page, max-width around 1080–1200px, spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop with generous left and right margins. Vertical spacing between major sections is 32–64px depending on importance (emergency instructions get more separation). Each component uses internal padding in 16–24px increments, preserving a light and airy feeling. There are no side-by-side columns; each block stretches the full width of the content column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is deliberately low-density: short headings, concise paragraphs, and vertically spaced lists, with generous line height and 16–24px vertical gaps between text elements. Critical instructions are visually grouped but not crowded, and the hotline list uses clean vertical rhythm with ample whitespace between entries. Forms and tabs are kept simple, with minimal fields and lots of breathing room between controls to avoid cognitive overload in a crisis context.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the logo and nav in the header to the centered 'Crisis Support' title in the hero, then down into the main content column. Within the column, the strongest attention is drawn first to the emergency instructions block at the top (slightly emphasized but still calm), then to the short intro text, then sequentially down through the hotline tabs, the filter form, and the hotline list. After scanning the list, the viewer naturally lands on the selected-hotlines summary and then the prominent safety-plan CTA button at the bottom. The flow is a gentle Z from header to hero, then a strong vertical reading path down the single column.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s standard header, a full-width hero band with a soft, blurred, nature-inspired background stretches edge-to-edge across the screen. Centered in this band, the 'Crisis Support' title appears in a large, calm serif type, sitting roughly in the vertical middle of the hero with generous top and bottom padding. Beneath the hero, the main content area begins after a noticeable vertical gap, presenting a single, centered column on a very pale green-tinted background that runs down the page. This column is constrained to a comfortable max width and aligned to the same center as the header logo and nav, leaving wide, quiet margins on each side. The first block in this column is a clearly framed emergency instructions section spanning the full width of the column, followed by a slightly smaller, explanatory intro section. Below that, hotline navigation tabs stretch across the column width, with the currently active tab subtly highlighted. Underneath, a softly outlined filter form allows gentle narrowing of hotline options, followed by a vertically stacked hotline list with plenty of breathing room between each entry. Near the bottom, a summary panel reiterates selected hotlines in a compact, calm block, and finally a centered, softly styled button leads to the Safety Plan page. The overall impression is of a clear, linear, reassuring layout where the most urgent information appears first, and the rest of the tools are calmly available as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Crisis Support' page title sits within the full-width hero band immediately below the global header. The hero extends horizontally from edge to edge of the viewport, while the title itself is centered both horizontally and approximately in the vertical middle of the hero area. The title text aligns to the central column used by the rest of the page content, but visually appears as a single, centered line (or two lines on smaller screens) set in a large serif type, with generous padding (approximately 64px–96px of space above and below within the hero). There are no other visible elements competing with this title in the hero; it is the singular focal point, floating over a soft, blurred green/nature background that subtly darkens behind the text to ensure readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element, anchored within the hero band and scrolling away naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main crisis-support container is positioned directly below the hero band, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of roughly 48px–64px. It forms a single centered column that defines the readable area for all subsequent components. On desktop, this container spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, with a max width of approximately 1080–1200px, and equal left/right margins creating wide, calm side gutters. The background behind this container is a very pale, green-tinted or off-white tone that extends full-width across the page, but the content itself remains constrained to the central column. Inside, the container provides consistent horizontal padding of 24px on both sides, and vertical padding of 32px at the top and bottom, establishing a soft, card-like feel without strong borders. All other sections (emergency instructions, intro, tabs, filters, list, summary, CTA) are stacked in order within this column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling container defining the main content column; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency instructions section is the first content block inside the main container, occupying the full width of the central column. It is positioned near the top of the container, with a modest top padding from the container edge (around 16–24px) but a more generous gap (32–40px) below it before the next section, emphasizing its priority. Visually, it appears as a slightly emphasized block: maintaining the same pale background or a subtly lighter panel, with 24px–32px of internal padding on all sides. Content inside is left-aligned, beginning with a clear, slightly larger heading (e.g., 'If you are in immediate danger') followed by short, line-broken instructions and possibly a small list (e.g., 'Call 911', 'Go to the nearest emergency room'), each separated vertically by 8–16px. The text is set with generous line height to preserve readability even under stress. There are no side columns or icons that break the single-column layout; everything is stacked vertically, creating a clear, strong block at the top of the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that scrolls with the page; visually emphasized solely through spacing, typography, and subtle background contrast.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The crisis intro section is placed directly below the emergency instructions section, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 32px. It stretches across the same full width of the central column and is visually lighter than the emergency block, using the base background without additional framing or with only minimal internal padding (around 16–24px). The text is left-aligned and consists of a brief heading (e.g., 'If you are not in immediate danger') followed by one or two short paragraphs explaining how to use the crisis resources and what to expect. Vertical spacing between heading and paragraphs is about 8–16px, and between paragraphs is similarly 8–16px, maintaining low density. This section reads as a calm explanatory bridge between the urgent instructions above and the hotline tools below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element with vertical stacking; no special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hotline view tabs sit below the crisis intro section with a vertical separation of approximately 32px–40px, marking the transition into the 'tools' part of the page. This tab navigation spans the full width of the main content column. The tab labels (e.g., 'All Hotlines', 'Local', 'National', 'Specialized') are aligned in a single horizontal row on desktop, starting from the left edge of the column and extending to the right, each tab receiving equal horizontal padding (about 16–24px) and vertical padding (8–12px). Tabs are visually understated: a soft underline or subtle background for the active tab, and muted text for inactive tabs, matching the calm aesthetic. On smaller screens, the tabs may wrap to a second line but still form part of the same full-width bar. There is comfortable white space (16–24px) above and below the tab row, preventing it from feeling cramped between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline navigation element that remains within the normal document flow and scrolls with the page; no sticky tab bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hotline filter form is positioned directly beneath the hotline view tabs, with a modest gap of about 16–24px. It occupies the full width of the central column and appears as a light, unobtrusive panel with gentle internal padding of 16–24px on all sides. All filter controls (dropdowns, checkboxes, search field) are arranged in a single vertical stack aligned to the left, preserving the single-column simplicity: for example, a label followed by a filter input, then a small vertical gap before the next filter group. On wider screens, controls may visually appear closer side-by-side but still read as part of one vertical block, without strict multi-column separation. There is ample vertical space (8–16px) between each field to maintain a calm rhythm. Any 'Clear filters' or 'Apply' actions sit at the bottom of the form, left-aligned with the fields, avoiding dense button clusters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard form block within the flow; expands vertically as needed with no fixed or floating elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hotline list appears immediately below the filter form, separated by roughly 24–32px to mark it as the main content area for the selected view and filters. It occupies the full width of the central column and extends vertically as needed. Each hotline entry is laid out as part of a single vertical list: entries are stacked one above the other with generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between them. Within each entry, the hotline name appears at the top, left-aligned, followed by a short description or eligibility notes, and then the phone number or access instructions, all aligned to the same left margin and separated by 4–8px vertical gaps. There is no multi-column layout; any tags or labels (e.g., '24/7', 'Text') appear inline or on the next line rather than in separate columns. The whole list maintains readable line lengths within the central column, and the bottom of the list transitions smoothly into the summary section with a larger vertical gap (32–40px) if the list is long, so the footer content doesn’t feel crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertically expanding list within the main column; scrolls as normal with no special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selected hotlines summary panel is placed below the hotline list, set off by a noticeable vertical space of around 32px–40px so it feels like a new, concluding block. It spans the full width of the central column. The panel has a slightly more defined, but still gentle, visual treatment: a very light contrasting background and possibly a subtle border or soft shadow, with 16–24px of internal padding. Inside, content is left-aligned and brief: a compact heading (e.g., 'Hotlines you’ve chosen for your safety plan') followed by a short list of the selected hotlines, each on its own line with 4–8px of vertical space between items. The summary is kept relatively shallow in height compared to the hotline list, functioning as a concise recap. There is additional space (24–32px) below this panel before the final CTA, emphasizing its role as a separate but related step before moving into safety planning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element; remains within the normal scrollable flow and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The safety plan CTA is the final element within the main container, positioned centrally beneath the selected hotlines summary panel with a generous vertical gap of approximately 32–48px, giving it room to stand out without feeling urgent or aggressive. The button itself is horizontally centered within the central column, not stretched full-width, and has ample padding (around 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally) following the spacing system. It may be accompanied by a short, single-line explanatory text just above it, left-aligned but within the same column, with a small 8–12px gap between that text and the button. There is substantial bottom padding (32–48px) below the button to the end of the page content, ensuring it does not feel cramped against the page footer. The button style is calm and solid, likely in a soft green tone consistent with the site’s palette, with clear but not harsh contrast.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard interactive element centered within the flow; no sticky/fixed behavior, simply reached as the user scrolls to the bottom of the page.

name: Safety Plan
title: Safety Plan
description: Page for creating, editing, viewing, and printing a personal safety plan with crisis contacts, warning signs, and coping strategies.

core_features: Load the current user's existing safety plan or a blank template with example prompts using getSafetyPlan when the page initializes.

Allow users to create and edit a list of crisis contacts, including a custom label and phone number for each entry, with the ability to add contacts from dynamically loaded crisis hotlines using getCrisisHotlines.
Provide editable lists for warning signs and coping strategies, using example prompts returned by getSafetyPlan to support users in filling out each list.
Save the safety plan (crisis contacts, warning signs, coping strategies) via saveSafetyPlan, and update the page state based on the save result.
Offer a read-only view of the saved safety plan for quick access, with options to switch to edit mode, trigger the browser print dialog, and navigate back to the Crisis Support page.
Support navigation back to crisis_support.html with URL parameters that preserve context (for example, indicating that the user arrived from the safety plan page).
user_workflows:
First-time safety plan creation:
1. User clicks the safety plan link from crisis_support.html and lands on safety_plan.html without specifying a mode.
2. The page initialization component calls getSafetyPlan.
3. If getSafetyPlan.hasExistingPlan is false, the page sets the mode to create (overriding any conflicting URL mode) and optionally updates the URL to mode=create.
4. The safety plan form is displayed with empty lists for crisis contacts, warning signs, and coping strategies, and shows example prompts from getSafetyPlan for warning signs and coping strategies.
5. In the crisis contacts section, the hotline suggestions panel calls getCrisisHotlines using any hotlineType and isLocal parameters found in the URL and displays the resulting hotlines.
6. The user fills in crisis contacts manually and/or clicks hotline entries to add them as contacts, then adds warning signs and coping strategies.
7. The user submits the form; the save handler calls saveSafetyPlan with the current crisisContacts, warningSigns, and copingStrategies from the form state.
8. If saveSafetyPlan.success is true, the page updates its internal SafetyPlan state with the returned safetyPlan, sets the mode to view (and may update the URL to mode=view&saveStatus=success), and optionally triggers printing if a print action was requested.
9. The user can then click a back link to navigate to crisis_support.html with a URL parameter indicating the return source.

Editing an existing safety plan:
1. A user with an existing safety plan navigates to safety_plan.html, optionally with mode=edit in the URL.
2. The page initialization component calls getSafetyPlan and receives hasExistingPlan set to true and a safetyPlan object.
3. If the URL mode parameter is edit, the safety plan form is displayed with all fields pre-populated from safetyPlan.crisis_contacts, safetyPlan.warning_signs, and safetyPlan.coping_strategies.
4. If the URL includes a section parameter (for example, warning_signs), the corresponding editor section is brought into focus or scrolled into view.
5. The user updates crisis contacts (adding, editing, or removing entries), warning signs, and coping strategies as desired.
6. The user submits the form; the save handler calls saveSafetyPlan with the updated lists.
7. On success, the page updates its SafetyPlan state with the returned safetyPlan, sets or keeps mode=view in the URL, sets saveStatus=success, and shows a confirmation message.
8. On error, the page sets saveStatus=error in the URL and displays an error message, allowing the user to retry without losing current form contents.

Quick view and print of an existing safety plan:
1. A user or staff member opens safety_plan.html with mode=view and optionally showPrint set in the URL.
2. The page initialization component calls getSafetyPlan. If hasExistingPlan is true, it loads the returned safetyPlan and sets mode=view (if not already set).
3. The read-only view panel displays the lists of crisis contacts, warning signs, and coping strategies from safetyPlan.
4. If the URL includes showPrint set to a truthy value, the view component triggers the browser print dialog once the safety plan is fully rendered and may then remove showPrint from the URL to prevent repeated print dialogs on refresh.
5. From the view mode, the user can click a button to switch to edit mode (which changes mode=edit in the URL) or navigate back to crisis_support.html with appropriate URL parameters.

Using hotline filters when building crisis contacts:
1. A user opens safety_plan.html with hotlineType and/or isLocal parameters in the URL.
2. The hotline suggestions panel calls getCrisisHotlines using these parameters to retrieve an appropriate set of hotline records.
3. The panel displays a list derived from the returned CrisisHotline items.
4. When the user selects a hotline, the crisis contact editor adds a new contact row with its label and phone populated from the selected hotline, allowing the user to further customize the label if desired.
5. The updated crisis contacts list is included when the user saves the safety plan using saveSafetyPlan.
interactions: On page load, the initialization logic parses URL parameters (including mode, hotlineType, isLocal, section, showPrint, saveStatus) and immediately calls getSafetyPlan. Based on hasExistingPlan and the mode parameter, it sets the initial page mode to create, edit, or view.
On page load or whenever hotlineType or isLocal parameters change in the URL, the hotline suggestions panel calls getCrisisHotlines with those parameters and refreshes the displayed list of hotlines.

When a user modifies any field in the crisis contacts, warning signs, or coping strategies editors, the form component updates its internal SafetyPlan draft state, keeping it in sync with user input until submission.
When a user clicks to add a new crisis contact row, the crisis-contact-list-editor component appends an empty contact entry to the draft crisisContacts list within the form state.
When a user clicks to remove a crisis contact row, the component removes that entry from the draft crisisContacts list and re-renders the list.

When a user clicks a hotline suggestion, the hotline-suggestions-panel emits an event with the selected CrisisHotline data; the crisis-contact-list-editor uses this data to create a new crisis contact entry and injects it into the draft crisisContacts list.

When a user adds or edits a warning sign line, the warning-signs-editor updates the draft warningSigns list; example prompts from getSafetyPlan.warningSignsExamples may be shown as optional suggestions or placeholders but are not stored unless the user chooses them.

When a user adds or edits a coping strategy line, the coping-strategies-editor updates the draft copingStrategies list; example prompts from getSafetyPlan.copingStrategiesExamples may be offered as suggestions.

When the safety plan form is submitted, the safety-plan-form component prevents default browser submission and calls saveSafetyPlan with the current draft crisisContacts, warningSigns, and copingStrategies lists.

After saveSafetyPlan resolves successfully, the save handler updates the displayed SafetyPlan data with the returned safetyPlan, changes the page mode to view, updates URL parameters (for example, mode=view&saveStatus=success), and notifies the notification-banner component to show a success message.

If saveSafetyPlan returns an error, the save handler sets saveStatus=error in the URL and passes the error message (if available) to the notification-banner, allowing the user to remain in edit mode with current draft data intact.

When a user clicks a control to switch between view and edit modes, the mode parameter in the URL is updated accordingly, and the page re-renders the appropriate components (view-only panel or editable form) while preserving the current SafetyPlan data in memory.

When a user clicks a control to focus a specific section (contacts, warning signs, or coping strategies), the section parameter in the URL is updated, and the section-anchor-navigation component scrolls or focuses the corresponding editor or view section.

When a user chooses to print the safety plan, either from view or edit mode, the page may update the showPrint parameter in the URL and immediately trigger the browser print dialog for the currently displayed safety plan content.

When a user clicks the back link to return to crisis_support.html, the navigation component redirects to crisis_support.html and may append a from parameter or similar context parameter to the URL to indicate that navigation originated from the safety plan page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters and server-side interfaces to manage state, rather than localStorage. The key URL parameters are: 
- mode: Controls whether the page is in create, edit, or view mode. Allowed values are interpreted as 'create', 'edit', or 'view'. On initial load, the page calls getSafetyPlan. If hasExistingPlan is false, the effective mode is forced to 'create', regardless of the URL, and the URL may be updated to mode=create for clarity. If hasExistingPlan is true and no mode is provided, the default effective mode is 'view'. User interactions (like clicking an edit control) update mode in the URL, which in turn affects which components are visible (form vs view-only panel).
- hotlineType and isLocal: These parameters are read on page initialization and whenever the URL changes. They are passed directly to getCrisisHotlines as hotlineType and isLocal, influencing which CrisisHotline entries are returned and shown in the hotline-suggestions-panel. Changing these parameters (for example, via filters in the UI that rewrite the URL) results in a new call to getCrisisHotlines.
- section: Indicates which part of the safety plan should be focused or scrolled into view ('contacts', 'warning_signs', or 'coping_strategies'). On load and on URL changes, the section-anchor-navigation component checks this value and ensures the appropriate section of the form or view is in focus. When the user navigates between sections in-page, the component updates the section parameter to match the selected section.
- showPrint: When present and truthy in the URL, this flag instructs the view component to trigger the browser print dialog once the SafetyPlan is loaded and rendered, typically when mode=view and getSafetyPlan.hasExistingPlan is true. After printing, the component can clean up the URL (e.g., by removing showPrint) via navigation logic, so subsequent refreshes do not automatically re-open the print dialog.
- saveStatus: Used to reflect the result of the most recent saveSafetyPlan call. On a successful save, the save handler sets saveStatus=success in the URL; on failure, it sets saveStatus=error. The notification-banner component reads this parameter to decide which message to show. The user can dismiss the notification, after which the component may update the URL to remove saveStatus. 
The main SafetyPlan data comes from getSafetyPlan on load and from saveSafetyPlan on save, and lives in memory while the page is open. Draft edits (unsaved changes) are kept in component state and are not persisted across full page reloads; only explicit saves via saveSafetyPlan update the stored safety plan. All shareable or restorable state that should persist across navigation (mode selection, section targeting, latest save status, hotline filters, and print behavior) is expressed via URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Safety Plan page by parsing URL parameters, calling getSafetyPlan to retrieve the current user's safety plan and example prompts, and determining the effective page mode (create, edit, or view). It also coordinates loading crisis hotline data when needed.
